> **Key Takeaway:** The sampling period $T$ is a **trade-off parameter** — small enough to capture system dynamics faithfully, large enough to permit real-time computation. KTU problems frequently test the **mapping, Jury stability, and practical selection rules** together.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling Discrete-Time Systems - Review of Z-Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT741 - DIGITAL CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modelling Discrete-Time Systems - Review of Z-Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Z-Transform

The **Z-Transform** is a mathematical operator that converts a discrete-time signal $x(k)$ (a sequence defined only at integer instants) into a complex frequency-domain representation $X(z)$. For the **Bilateral (Two-Sided) Z-Transform** used in discrete-time system analysis, the defining equation is:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k)\, z^{-k}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable of the form $z = re^{j\omega}$, and $k$ is the discrete-time index.

In the context of **Digital Control Systems** (KTU PEICT741), we deal almost exclusively with **causal signals** that begin at $k=0$. For such signals, the **Unilateral (One-Sided) Z-Transform** is used:

$$X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x(k)\} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(k)\, z^{-k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The unilateral Z-transform is the **default tool** for analysing causal LTI discrete-time systems, solving difference equations, and deriving pulse transfer functions. Master the one-sided form thoroughly.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "DNA Sequencing" of Signals

Imagine a **musical recording** as a sequence of amplitude values sampled at every millisecond. The Z-Transform acts like a **spectrometer for digital signals**:

- Just as a prism splits white light into its constituent colours, the Z-Transform decomposes a discrete signal $x(k)$ into its **inherent frequency components** weighted by the complex variable $z$.
- The complex variable $z = re^{j\omega}$ has two parts:
  - **Magnitude $r$** $\rightarrow$ describes growth or decay (the "envelope" of the signal).
  - **Angle $\omega$** $\rightarrow$ describes oscillation (the "rhythm" of the signal).
- When you set $r = 1$ (i.e., $z = e^{j\omega}$), the Z-Transform reduces to the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** — analogous to how white light through a prism gives the visible spectrum on the unit circle.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Region of Convergence (ROC):** The set of all $z$ values in the complex plane for which the summation converges to a finite value. The ROC uniquely determines which time-domain sequence produced a given $X(z)$ — different signals can have identical $X(z)$ but different ROCs.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling period | $T$ | Engineering parameter (seconds) |
| Sampling frequency | $f_s$ | $\geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) |
| Angular sampling frequency | $\omega_s$ | $2\pi/T$ rad/sample |
| Unit circle in z-plane | $\vert z \vert$ | $= 1$ |
| Stability boundary | $\vert z \vert$ | $= 1$ (Bilinear map of $j\omega$-axis) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Z-Transform is the **backbone of digital controller design**. Every digital PID, lead-lag compensator, and Kalman filter implemented on a microcontroller, DSP, or PLC is designed and analysed in the z-plane. Automotive ECUs, smartphone gyroscopes, and industrial robotics all rely on Z-domain mathematics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need the Z-Transform?

Discrete-time systems are described by **difference equations**:

$$y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) + a_2 y(k-2) = b_0 u(k) + b_1 u(k-1)$$

In the time domain, solving this requires cumbersome iteration. The Z-Transform converts the difference equation into an **algebraic equation** in $z$, allowing closed-form solution and frequency-domain insight — exactly as the Laplace Transform does for continuous systems.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Z-Transform Evaluation

The Z-Transform evaluation follows a structured pipeline:

1. **Identify** the discrete-time signal $x(k)$ and its range (causal $\rightarrow k \geq 0$; anti-causal $\rightarrow k < 0$).
2. **Substitute** the signal expression into the summation $\sum x(k) z^{-k}$.
3. **Apply** the geometric series result: $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^k = \dfrac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$.
4. **Express** the result in closed form as a rational function $X(z) = \dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)}$.
5. **Determine** the Region of Convergence (ROC) from convergence constraints.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Z-Transform Reference Table

### 2.3.1 Z-Transforms of Standard Test Signals

| Signal $x(k)$ | Z-Transform $X(z)$ | Region of Convergence (ROC) |
|---|---|---|
| $\delta(k)$ (Unit Impulse) | $1$ | All $z$ |
| $u(k)$ (Unit Step) | $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^k u(k)$ (Exponential) | $\dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $k \cdot u(k)$ (Ramp) | $\dfrac{Tz}{(z-1)^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $k^2 u(k)$ (Parabola) | $\dfrac{T^2 z(z+1)}{(z-1)^3}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $\sin(\omega k) \cdot u(k)$ | $\dfrac{z \sin\omega}{z^2 - 2z\cos\omega + 1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $\cos(\omega k) \cdot u(k)$ | $\dfrac{z(z-\cos\omega)}{z^2 - 2z\cos\omega + 1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^k \sin(\omega k) u(k)$ | $\dfrac{az\sin\omega}{z^2 - 2az\cos\omega + a^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $a^k \cos(\omega k) u(k)$ | $\dfrac{z(z - a\cos\omega)}{z^2 - 2az\cos\omega + a^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |

### 2.3.2 Key Properties of the Z-Transform

| Property | Time Domain | Z-Domain | ROC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linearity | $a x_1(k) + b x_2(k)$ | $a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$ | $R_1 \cap R_2$ |
| Time Shifting | $x(k - n)$ | $z^{-n} X(z)$ | $R$ (excluding $z=0$ if $n>0$) |
| Scaling in z | $a^k x(k)$ | $X(z/a)$ | $\vert a \vert R$ |
| Differentiation | $k x(k)$ | $-z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | $R$ |
| Convolution | $x_1(k) * x_2(k)$ | $X_1(z) X_2(z)$ | $R_1 \cap R_2$ |
| Multiplication by $a^k$ | $a^k x(k)$ | $X(z/a)$ | Scaled ROC |
| Initial Value | $x(0)$ | $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | — |
| Final Value | $\lim_{k \to \infty} x(k)$ | $\lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ | Requires poles inside unit circle except at $z=1$ |

### 2.3.3 Critical Theorems

**Initial Value Theorem (IVT):**
$$x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

**Final Value Theorem (FVT):**
$$\lim_{k \to \infty} x(k) = \lim_{z \to 1} \left[(z-1) X(z)\right]$$

> Valid only if all poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$, except possibly a simple pole at $z=1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The FVT is a **favourite exam topic**. The most common mistake is forgetting the ROC/stability check. If the system is unstable, the FVT gives misleading results.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Z-Transform of the Unit Step Function $u(k)$

We define $u(k) = 1$ for $k \geq 0$ and $u(k) = 0$ for $k < 0$. Substituting into the unilateral Z-Transform:

$$U(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} u(k) \, z^{-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 1 \cdot z^{-k}$$

Rewriting the summation explicitly:

$$U(z) = 1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + z^{-3} + \cdots$$

This is an **infinite geometric series** with first term $a = 1$ and common ratio $r = z^{-1}$. The sum converges when $\vert z^{-1} \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert > 1$.

Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^k = \dfrac{1}{1-r}$:

$$U(z) = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $z$ to obtain the standard rational form:

$$\boxed{U(z) = \frac{z}{z-1}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Z-Transform of the Exponential Signal $a^k u(k)$

We define $x(k) = a^k$ for $k \geq 0$ and $x(k) = 0$ for $k < 0$. Substituting:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} a^k \, z^{-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{a}{z}\right)^k$$

For convergence, we need $\left\vert \dfrac{a}{z} \right\vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

Applying the geometric series formula with $r = a/z$:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{a}{z}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{z-a}{z}} = \frac{z}{z-a}$$

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z}{z-a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Z-Transform of the Ramp Signal $k \cdot u(k)$

We define $x(k) = k$ for $k \geq 0$. Directly summing would be cumbersome, so we use the **Differentiation Property**:

$$X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{k \cdot u(k)\} = -z \frac{d}{dz} \left[ \mathcal{Z}\{u(k)\} \right]$$

From Derivation 1, $U(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$. Differentiating:

$$\frac{dU(z)}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\left(\frac{z}{z-1}\right) = \frac{(z-1)(1) - z(1)}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{-1}{(z-1)^2}$$

Therefore:

$$X(z) = -z \cdot \frac{-1}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}$$

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Z-Transform of $\sin(\omega k) \cdot u(k)$

We use **Euler's identity** to express sine in terms of exponentials:

$$\sin(\omega k) = \frac{e^{j\omega k} - e^{-j\omega k}}{2j}$$

Applying linearity and the exponential result from Derivation 2:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{\sin(\omega k) u(k)\} = \frac{1}{2j}\left[ \frac{z}{z - e^{j\omega}} - \frac{z}{z - e^{-j\omega}} \right]$$

Combining over a common denominator:

$$= \frac{z}{2j} \cdot \frac{(z - e^{-j\omega}) - (z - e^{j\omega})}{(z - e^{j\omega})(z - e^{-j\omega})}$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$(z - e^{-j\omega}) - (z - e^{j\omega}) = e^{j\omega} - e^{-j\omega} = 2j\sin\omega$$

Simplifying the denominator (using $e^{j\omega} \cdot e^{-j\omega} = 1$):

$$(z - e^{j\omega})(z - e^{-j\omega}) = z^2 - z(e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}) + 1 = z^2 - 2z\cos\omega + 1$$

Substituting back:

$$X(z) = \frac{z}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\sin\omega}{z^2 - 2z\cos\omega + 1}$$

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z\sin\omega}{z^2 - 2z\cos\omega + 1}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Z-Transform of a Delayed Sequence $x(k-n) u(k-n)$

We apply the **Time-Shifting Property**. Starting from:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(k) z^{-k}$$

For the delayed sequence $y(k) = x(k-n) u(k-n)$, the sum index shifts:

$$Y(z) = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} x(k-n) z^{-k}$$

Let $m = k - n$, so $k = m + n$ and $dk = dm$:

$$Y(z) = \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} x(m) z^{-(m+n)} = z^{-n} \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} x(m) z^{-m}$$

$$\boxed{Y(z) = z^{-n} X(z), \quad \text{ROC: same as } X(z) \text{ (excluding } z=0 \text{)}}$$

## 3.6 Inverse Z-Transform — Partial Fraction Expansion Method

Given $X(z) = \dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)}$, the standard procedure is:

1. **Divide** if the order of $N(z) \geq$ order of $D(z)$ (improper fraction).
2. **Express** the remainder as partial fractions:
   $$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{A_1}{z - p_1} + \frac{A_2}{z - p_2} + \cdots + \frac{A_n}{z - p_n}$$
3. **Multiply** both sides by $z$ to get $X(z)$ back.
4. **Match** each term with the standard form $\dfrac{z}{z-p_i}$, which inverts to $p_i^k u(k)$.
5. **Reconstruct** $x(k) = A_1 p_1^k + A_2 p_2^k + \cdots$ (for causal ROC).

### Worked Example: Inverse Z-Transform

Find $x(k)$ given $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2 + z}{(z-1)(z-2)}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 2$.

**Step 1: Perform partial fraction expansion of $X(z)/z$:**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z+1}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z-2}$$

**Step 2: Solve for residues:**

$$A = \left.\frac{z+1}{z-2}\right|_{z=1} = \frac{1+1}{1-2} = \frac{2}{-1} = -2$$

$$B = \left.\frac{z+1}{z-1}\right|_{z=2} = \frac{2+1}{2-1} = 3$$

**Step 3: Reconstruct $X(z)$:**

$$X(z) = \frac{-2z}{z-1} + \frac{3z}{z-2}$$

**Step 4: Apply inverse Z-Transform** using $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z}{z-a}\right\} = a^k u(k)$:

$$x(k) = \left[-2(1)^k + 3(2)^k\right] u(k) = \left(-2 + 3 \cdot 2^k\right) u(k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification at $k=0$:** $x(0) = -2 + 3(1) = 1$. From the original sequence at $k=0$: $X(z)$ at $z \to \infty$ gives $1$. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Z-Transform as a Bridge Between Domains

The Z-Transform acts as a bi-directional operator connecting the discrete-time domain and the complex frequency domain. The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the conceptual flow:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Discrete Time Signal x of k"] --> B["Apply Z Transform Sum x k z to the minus k"]
    B --> C["Complex Frequency Domain X of z"]
    C --> D{"ROC exists and is valid"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Analyse System Poles and Zeros"]
    D -- No --> F["Signal or system is non physical"]
    E --> G["Determine Stability and Response"]
    G --> H["Apply Inverse Z Transform via PFE or Long Division"]
    H --> I["Reconstruct Time Domain x of k"]
    I --> J["Plot or Implement on Hardware DSP MCU FPGA"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Discrete-Time Control System using Z-Transform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Reference Input R of z"] --> S1["Summing Junction"]
    S1 --> C["Digital Controller D of z"]
    C --> H1["Zero Order Hold ZOH"]
    H1 --> P["Plant G of s Continuous"]
    P --> Y["Continuous Output y of t"]
    Y --> SAMP["Sampler with period T"]
    SAMP --> FB["Feedback Unit Delay z to the minus 1"]
    FB --> S1
    SAMP --> YD["Discrete Output Y of z"]
    YD --> OUT["Display or Actuator Driver"]
```

## 4.3 Mapping Between s-plane and z-plane (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["s plane Left Half stable region Re s less than 0"] --> B["Bilinear Mapping z equals e to the sT"]
    B --> C["z plane Interior of Unit Circle mod z less than 1"]
    D["s plane Imaginary Axis j omega axis"] --> B
    B --> E["z plane Unit Circle mod z equals 1"]
    F["s plane Right Half unstable Re s greater than 0"] --> B
    B --> G["z plane Exterior of Unit Circle mod z greater than 1"]
```

## 4.4 Inverse Z-Transform Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Given X of z as rational function"] --> B{"Is X z proper degree N less than degree D"}
    B -- No --> C["Perform Long Division to extract polynomial part"]
    B -- Yes --> D["Go directly to PFE"]
    C --> E["Divide to get quotient Q z and remainder R z over D z"]
    E --> F["Express R z over D z using PFE"]
    D --> F
    F --> G{"Identify ROC from problem statement"}
    G -- Causal ROC outside outermost pole --> H["Right sided sequence x k for k greater or equal 0"]
    G -- Anti causal ROC inside innermost pole --> I["Left sided sequence x k for k less than 0"]
    G -- Two sided ROC annular --> J["Two sided sequence both positive and negative k"]
    H --> K["Apply table lookup a to the k gives z over z minus a"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L["Final Answer x of k"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip:** Always sketch the pole-zero plot in the z-plane. Poles **inside** the unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$ indicate a **stable** causal system. Poles on the unit circle indicate **marginally stable** (oscillatory) systems. Poles outside indicate **unstable** systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Z-Transform of a discrete-time signal. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The **Z-Transform** of a discrete-time signal $x(k)$ is defined as:

$$X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x(k)\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) z^{-k}$$

For **causal signals** (which start at $k=0$), the **unilateral (one-sided) Z-Transform** is used:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(k) z^{-k}$$

where $z = re^{j\omega}$ is a complex variable. The Z-Transform converts a discrete-time sequence into a complex frequency-domain representation, enabling the analysis of discrete-time systems through algebraic manipulation rather than iterative difference-equation solving. The set of $z$ values for which the series converges is called the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

### Question 2: State and explain the Final Value Theorem of Z-Transform. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **Final Value Theorem (FVT)** states that if $x(k)$ has a Z-Transform $X(z)$ and $\lim_{k \to \infty} x(k)$ exists, then:

$$\lim_{k \to \infty} x(k) = \lim_{z \to 1} \left[(z-1) X(z)\right]$$

**Conditions for validity:**
1. The function $(z-1)X(z)$ must have **all its poles strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$, **except possibly a simple pole at $z=1$**.
2. The system must be **stable** (causal and all poles inside unit circle).

**Engineering Utility:** FVT is used to determine the **steady-state error** and **steady-state output** of digital control systems without computing the full time response. For example, if the system is Type-1, the FVT gives the position error constant directly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### Part (a): Derive the Z-Transform of the unit step function $u(k)$ and the exponential signal $a^k u(k)$. Mention the ROC in each case. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Z-Transform of $u(k)$**

$$U(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} u(k) z^{-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} z^{-k} = 1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + \cdots$$

This is a geometric series with ratio $z^{-1}$. It converges when $\vert z^{-1} \vert < 1 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert > 1$.

$$U(z) = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z-1}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$$

**Step 2: Z-Transform of $a^k u(k)$**

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} a^k z^{-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{a}{z}\right)^k$$

Convergence requires $\left\vert \dfrac{a}{z} \right\vert < 1 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{a}{z}} = \frac{z}{z - a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

#### Part (b): Find the inverse Z-Transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{3z^2 + 2z + 1}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)}$ assuming the ROC is $\vert z \vert > 3$. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Express $X(z)/z$ in partial fractions:**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{3z^2 + 2z + 1}{z(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)} = \frac{A}{z} + \frac{B}{z-1} + \frac{C}{z-2} + \frac{D}{z-3}$$

**Step 2: Compute residues using the cover-up method:**

$$A = \left.\frac{3z^2 + 2z + 1}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)}\right|_{z=0} = \frac{1}{(-1)(-2)(-3)} = \frac{1}{-6} = -\frac{1}{6}$$

$$B = \left.\frac{3z^2 + 2z + 1}{z(z-2)(z-3)}\right|_{z=1} = \frac{3+2+1}{1 \cdot (-1) \cdot (-2)} = \frac{6}{2} = 3$$

$$C = \left.\frac{3z^2 + 2z + 1}{z(z-1)(z-3)}\right|_{z=2} = \frac{12+4+1}{2 \cdot 1 \cdot (-1)} = \frac{17}{-2} = -\frac{17}{2}$$

$$D = \left.\frac{3z^2 + 2z + 1}{z(z-1)(z-2)}\right|_{z=3} = \frac{27+6+1}{3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1} = \frac{34}{6} = \frac{17}{3}$$

**Step 3: Reconstruct $X(z)$ by multiplying by $z$:**

$$X(z) = -\frac{1}{6} + \frac{3z}{z-1} - \frac{17z}{2(z-2)} + \frac{17z}{3(z-3)}$$

**Step 4: Apply inverse Z-Transform** using $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z}{z-p}\right\} = p^k u(k)$ and $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{1\} = \delta(k)$:

$$x(k) = -\frac{1}{6}\delta(k) + \left[3 - \frac{17}{2}(2)^k + \frac{17}{3}(3)^k\right] u(k)$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a): State and prove the time-shifting and convolution properties of the Z-Transform. (7 Marks)

**Property 1: Time Shifting**

**Statement:** If $\mathcal{Z}\{x(k)\} = X(z)$ with ROC $R$, then:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{x(k-n)u(k-n)\} = z^{-n} X(z)$$

**Proof:**

$$\mathcal{Z}\{x(k-n)u(k-n)\} = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} x(k-n) z^{-k}$$

Let $m = k - n$, so $k = m + n$:

$$= \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} x(m) z^{-(m+n)} = z^{-n} \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} x(m) z^{-m} = z^{-n} X(z)$$

**ROC:** Same as $R$ except $z = 0$ is excluded if $n > 0$.

**Property 2: Convolution**

**Statement:** If $x_1(k) * x_2(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x_1(n) x_2(k-n)$, then:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{x_1(k) * x_2(k)\} = X_1(z) X_2(z)$$

**Proof:**

$$\mathcal{Z}\{x_1(k) * x_2(k)\} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left[\sum_{n=0}^{k} x_1(n) x_2(k-n)\right] z^{-k}$$

Interchanging the order of summation (with $m = k - n$):

$$= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x_1(n) \left[\sum_{m=0}^{\infty} x_2(m) z^{-m}\right] z^{-n} = X_2(z) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x_1(n) z^{-n} = X_1(z) X_2(z)$$

**ROC:** $R_1 \cap R_2$

#### Part (b): Using the Z-Transform, solve the difference equation $y(k) - 3y(k-1) + 2y(k-2) = u(k)$ with $y(-1) = 0$, $y(-2) = 0$. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Take unilateral Z-Transform of both sides:**

$$\mathcal{Z}\{y(k)\} - 3\mathcal{Z}\{y(k-1)\} + 2\mathcal{Z}\{y(k-2)\} = \mathcal{Z}\{u(k)\}$$

**Step 2: Apply time-shifting property with initial conditions:**

Recall that for unilateral Z-Transform:
- $\mathcal{Z}\{y(k-1)\} = z^{-1}Y(z) + y(-1)$
- $\mathcal{Z}\{y(k-2)\} = z^{-2}Y(z) + z^{-1}y(-1) + y(-2)$

With $y(-1) = y(-2) = 0$:

$$Y(z) - 3 z^{-1} Y(z) + 2 z^{-2} Y(z) = \frac{z}{z-1}$$

**Step 3: Factor and solve for $Y(z)$:**

$$Y(z) \left[1 - 3z^{-1} + 2z^{-2}\right] = \frac{z}{z-1}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $z^2$:

$$Y(z) \cdot \frac{z^2 - 3z + 2}{z^2} = \frac{z}{z-1}$$

$$Y(z) = \frac{z^3}{(z-1)(z^2 - 3z + 2)} = \frac{z^3}{(z-1)(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{z^3}{(z-1)^2(z-2)}$$

**Step 4: Partial Fraction Expansion of $Y(z)/z$:**

$$\frac{Y(z)}{z} = \frac{z^2}{(z-1)^2(z-2)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{(z-1)^2} + \frac{C}{z-2}$$

Computing residues:

$$B = \left.\frac{z^2}{z-2}\right|_{z=1} = \frac{1}{-1} = -1$$

$$C = \left.\frac{z^2}{(z-1)^2}\right|_{z=2} = \frac{4}{1} = 4$$

For $A$: Multiply both sides by $z$ and differentiate, or use algebraic balance:

$$A = \left.\frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{z^2}{z-2}\right]\right|_{z=1} = \left.\frac{2z(z-2) - z^2}{(z-2)^2}\right|_{z=1} = \frac{2(-1) - 1}{1} = -3$$

**Step 5: Reconstruct $Y(z)$ and invert:**

$$Y(z) = \frac{-3z}{z-1} + \frac{-z}{(z-1)^2} + \frac{4z}{z-2}$$

Using inverse Z-transform pairs:
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z}{z-1}\right\} = 1$
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z}{(z-1)^2}\right\} = k$
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z}{z-2}\right\} = 2^k$

$$\boxed{y(k) = \left[-3 - k + 4(2)^k\right] u(k)}$$

**Verification at $k=0$:** $y(0) = -3 - 0 + 4(1) = 1$ ✓ (since $y(-1) = y(-2) = 0$ and the input starts at $k=0$, the output should rise from 1).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting ROC:** Always state the ROC explicitly. Marks are deducted if the ROC is missing or incorrect.
> 2. **Sign errors in partial fractions:** Carefully compute residues. A single sign error cascades through the entire answer.
> 3. **Confusing bilateral vs. unilateral transform:** In control problems with initial conditions, the **unilateral** Z-Transform is mandatory. Mixing them up leads to incorrect difference-equation solutions.
> 4. **FVT without stability check:** If a question asks for steady-state value, **always verify stability** before applying the FVT. KTU examiners specifically look for this step.
> 5. **Poles inside vs. outside unit circle:** A causal LTI system is **stable if and only if all poles are strictly inside the unit circle**. State this clearly when relevant.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Z-Transform Fundamentals**

- ✅ **Z-Transform** converts discrete-time signals to complex frequency domain: $X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(k) z^{-k}$.
- ✅ **ROC** must always be specified; it uniquely determines the time-domain sequence from $X(z)$.
- ✅ **Standard test signals to memorize:** $\delta(k) \to 1$, $u(k) \to \dfrac{z}{z-1}$, $a^k u(k) \to \dfrac{z}{z-a}$.
- ✅ **Trigonometric transforms:** $\sin(\omega k)$ and $\cos(\omega k)$ forms are high-yield for KTU problems.
- ✅ **Time-shifting property:** Delay by $n$ samples multiplies $X(z)$ by $z^{-n}$.
- ✅ **Convolution in time ↔ Multiplication in z-domain.** This is the foundation of digital filter design.
- ✅ **IVT:** $x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ — useful for quick sanity check.
- ✅ **FVT:** $\lim_{k \to \infty} x(k) = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)X(z)$ — requires all poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ inside the unit circle.
- ✅ **Stability criterion:** A causal LTI discrete system is **stable** if and only if **all poles of $X(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$.
- ✅ **Inverse Z-Transform methods:** (i) Direct table lookup, (ii) Partial Fraction Expansion, (iii) Long Division (power series), (iv) Residue method.
- ✅ **Connection to Laplace:** $z = e^{sT}$ — the s-plane left half maps to the z-plane unit circle interior; the $j\omega$-axis maps to the unit circle itself.
- ✅ **Unilateral Z-Transform** includes initial conditions and is the **default** in KTU digital control problems.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** The Z-Transform is the **single most important tool** in Digital Control. Every subsequent module — pulse transfer functions, stability analysis (Jury's test, bilinear transform), and digital controller design (Deadbeat, Dahlin, Pole-Placement) — depends on the concepts covered in this Module 1 review. Master the derivations, table lookups, and inverse-transform techniques thoroughly before moving forward.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping of S-Plane to Z-Plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT741 - DIGITAL CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Mapping of S-Plane to Z-Plane ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **S-Plane to Z-Plane Mapping (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):** > The mapping between the **s-plane** (continuous-time frequency domain) and the **z-plane** (discrete-time frequency domain) is governed by the fundamental transformation relation: > $$z = e^{sT}$$ > where **$s = \sigma + j\omega$** is the complex Laplace variable and **$T$** is the **sampling period** (in seconds). This exponential relationship bridges continuous-time control theory (LCCDE → Laplace) with discrete-time digital control (Difference Equations → Z-Transform). This single equation is the cornerstone of all digital control system analysis. It allows the engineer to "translate" the well-known stability boundaries, transient response characteristics, and frequency response contours of the s-plane into their discrete-time equivalents on the z-plane. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Rotating Spotlight":** > Imagine a lighthouse beam rotating at a constant speed. The lighthouse sits on a circular platform representing the **unit circle** ($|z|=1$). The beam illuminates points on a vast curtain (the s-plane) at regular time intervals. As the beam sweeps, it samples the continuous curtain at discrete moments. > - The **rotation angle** corresponds to the **digital frequency** ($\omega T$). > - The **radial distance** of the lit point from the lighthouse represents the **decay** ($e^{\sigma T}$). > - Points **closer to the lighthouse** have decayed strongly (stable, negative $\sigma$). > - Points **far from the lighthouse** are exploding outward (unstable, positive $\sigma$). In simpler terms: The **z-plane is a "folded" version of the s-plane**, where infinite horizontal strips of the s-plane are "stacked" on top of one another, all collapsing into a single unit circle. Only one strip — the **primary strip** — is uniquely mapped. ### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Engineering Value (KTU) | |---|---|---| | **$T$** | Sampling period | **$0.1$ s to $1.0$ s** for process control | | **$f_s = 1/T$** | Sampling frequency | Must be $\geq 2 \times$ signal bandwidth (**Nyquist Rate**) | | **$\omega_s = 2\pi/T$** | Sampling angular frequency | Defines the **Primary Strip** boundary | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The mapping is **not one-to-one** between the entire s-plane and the z-plane. The mapping is *many-to-one*: infinitely many points in the s-plane map to a *single* point in the z-plane. Only the **Primary Strip** (defined by $-\pi/T \leq \omega \leq \pi/T$) has a unique correspondence. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown The exponential mapping $z = e^{sT}$ can be decomposed as follows: - **Step 1 — Decompose the s-variable:** Express $s$ in its rectangular form: $s = \sigma + j\omega$. - **Step 2 — Substitute into the mapping equation:** $$z = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T} = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$$ - **Step 3 — Convert to polar form (modulus and argument):** $$\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}, \quad \angle z = \omega T$$ - **Step 4 — Interpret the geometric meaning:** - The **modulus** $\vert z \vert$ depends *only* on the real part $\sigma$. - The **angle** $\angle z$ depends *only* on the imaginary part $\omega$ (modulo $2\pi$). - **Step 5 — Identify the regions of stability:** - $\sigma < 0$ (Left Half Plane, LHP) $\Rightarrow \vert z \vert < 1$ $\Rightarrow$ **Inside the unit circle** $\Rightarrow$ Stable discrete poles. - $\sigma = 0$ (Imaginary axis) $\Rightarrow \vert z \vert = 1$ $\Rightarrow$ **On the unit circle** $\Rightarrow$ Marginally stable. - $\sigma > 0$ (Right Half Plane, RHP) $\Rightarrow \vert z \vert > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ **Outside the unit circle** $\Rightarrow$ Unstable. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Mapping The transformation $z = e^{sT}$ arises naturally from **impulse train sampling** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$: $$x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$$ Taking the Laplace transform and the subsequent Z-transform (using the relationship $X(z) = \sum x(kT)\,z^{-k}$) yields the direct exponential substitution $z = e^{sT}$. This is **not an approximation** — it is an *exact* representation of the sampled-data system. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Use `\vert` for absolute values in tables to preserve markdown structure.** | # | Concept | Formula | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fundamental Mapping | $z = e^{sT}$ | Bridges continuous and discrete domains | | 2 | Inverse Mapping | $s = \dfrac{1}{T} \ln(z)$ | Used in digital redesign | | 3 | Magnitude of $z$ | $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ | Decay/growth rate of discrete pole | | 4 | Argument of $z$ | $\angle z = \omega T$ | Discrete frequency in radians | | 5 | Primary Strip | $-\pi/T \leq \omega \leq \pi/T$ | Uniquely mapped region | | 6 | LHP $\to$ Unit Disk | $\sigma < 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert < 1$ | Discrete-time stability | | 7 | $j\omega$-axis $\to$ Unit Circle | $\sigma = 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert = 1$ | Oscillatory boundary | | 8 | Constant $\zeta$ Locus | $z = \exp\!\left[\left(-\zeta \pm j\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}\right)\omega_n T\right]$ | Logarithmic spiral | | 9 | Constant $\omega_n$ Locus | $z = \exp\!\left[(-\zeta \omega_n + j\omega_n)T\right]$ as $\zeta$ varies | Radial ray from origin | | 10 | Sampling Relation | $\omega_d = \dfrac{2\pi k}{N} \pm \omega$ | Frequency aliasing equation | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Digital Redesign of Analog Controllers:** Engineers design a controller in the s-plane (using familiar Bode/Nyquist techniques) and then apply the mapping to implement it in a microcontroller/DSP. - **Stability Analysis:** A digital system is **stable if and only if** all poles lie strictly **inside the unit circle** (the discrete analog of the LHP). - **Anti-Aliasing Filter Design:** The primary strip defines the maximum useful analog frequency $\omega_s/2$, dictating analog pre-filter cutoff frequencies. - **PID Tuning in Digital Domain:** Sampling rate directly affects how the s-plane pole locations (e.g., $\omega_n$, $\zeta$) translate to digital implementations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Magnitude and Angle of $z$ from $s$ **Given:** $z = e^{sT}$, where $s = \sigma + j\omega$. **Step 1.** Substitute $s$ into the exponent: $$z = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T}$$ **Step 2.** Split the exponential using $e^{a+b} = e^a \cdot e^b$: $$z = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$$ **Step 3.** Apply Euler's identity, $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$: $$z = e^{\sigma T}\bigl[\cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)\bigr]$$ **Step 4.** Compute the modulus: $$\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}\,\sqrt{\cos^2(\omega T) + \sin^2(\omega T)} = e^{\sigma T}$$ **Step 5.** Compute the argument: $$\angle z = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sin(\omega T)}{\cos(\omega T)}\right) = \omega T$$ > **Result:** The real part of $s$ controls the *radial distance* from the origin in the z-plane, while the imaginary part of $s$ controls the *angular sweep* around the origin. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mapping of the Imaginary Axis (Constant $\sigma = 0$) **Given:** $s = j\omega$ (vary $\omega$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$). **Step 1.** Substitute into mapping: $$z = e^{j\omega T} = \cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)$$ **Step 2.** Compute the modulus: $$\vert z \vert = \sqrt{\cos^2(\omega T) + \sin^2(\omega T)} = 1$$ **Step 3.** Therefore, as $\omega$ sweeps from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, the locus of $z$ is the **unit circle**, traced infinitely many times. **Step 4.** Identify the period of repetition: The argument $\angle z = \omega T$ increases by $2\pi$ when $\omega$ increases by $2\pi/T \equiv \omega_s$ (sampling frequency). > **Result:** The imaginary axis of the s-plane (the stability boundary for continuous systems) maps to the **unit circle** of the z-plane (the stability boundary for discrete systems). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mapping of Constant Damping Ratio ($\zeta$) Lines **Given:** Lines in the s-plane defined by $s = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_d$, where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ and $\zeta$ is held constant while $\omega_n$ varies. **Step 1.** Substitute into the mapping: $$z = e^{(-\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_d)T}$$ **Step 2.** Separate the exponential: $$z = e^{-\zeta\omega_n T} \cdot e^{j\omega_d T}$$ **Step 3.** Take the natural logarithm of both sides to obtain a parametric form: $$\ln(z) = -\zeta\omega_n T + j\omega_d T = \omega_n T\left(-\zeta + j\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}\right)$$ **Step 4.** Let $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$, so that $\zeta = \cos\phi$ and $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = \sin\phi$. Therefore: $$\ln(z) = \omega_n T\left(-\cos\phi + j\sin\phi\right) = \omega_n T \cdot e^{j(\pi - \phi)}$$ **Step 5.** Parametrically: The real part of $\ln z$ is $\omega_n T \cos(\pi - \phi) = -\omega_n T \zeta$, and the imaginary part is $\omega_n T \sin(\pi - \phi) = \omega_n T \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$. **Step 6.** The argument of $\ln(z)$ is constant at $(\pi - \phi)$, meaning $\ln(z)$ moves along a **ray** from the origin in the $\ln(z)$-plane. **Step 7.** A ray in the $\ln(z)$-plane corresponds to a **logarithmic spiral** in the z-plane: $$z = \exp\!\left[\omega_n T \cdot e^{j(\pi - \phi)}\right]$$ > **Result:** A constant-$\zeta$ line in the s-plane becomes a **logarithmic spiral** originating at $z=1$ and unwinding as $\omega_n$ increases, always terminating on the positive real axis at $\omega_n = 0$. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Mapping of Constant Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$) Lines **Given:** Lines in the s-plane defined by $s = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_d$, where $\omega_n$ is held constant while $\zeta$ varies from $0$ to $1$. **Step 1.** Substitute into the mapping: $$z = e^{(-\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})T}$$ **Step 2.** Compute the modulus: $$\vert z \vert = e^{-\zeta\omega_n T}$$ **Step 3.** As $\zeta \to 0$ (no damping), $\vert z \vert \to 1$ (point lies on the unit circle). **Step 4.** As $\zeta \to 1$ (critical damping), $\omega_d \to 0$ and $\vert z \vert = e^{-\omega_n T}$ (point lies on the negative real axis... wait, it lies on the positive real axis at $e^{-\omega_n T}$). **Step 5.** Compute the argument: $$\angle z = \omega_n T\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$ **Step 6.** As $\zeta$ increases from $0$ to $1$, the angle decreases from $\omega_n T$ to $0$. > **Result:** A constant-$\omega_n$ line in the s-plane becomes a **radial ray** in the z-plane starting at $z = 1$ (when $\zeta = 0$) and spiraling inward (or outward, depending on context) as $\zeta$ increases. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5: The Primary Strip Width and Aliasing **Given:** The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ is periodic in $\omega$ with period $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$. **Step 1.** The argument of $z$ is $\omega T$. The argument is *invariant* under: $$\omega \to \omega + \dfrac{2\pi k}{T}, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$ **Step 2.** This means an infinite lattice of horizontal strips in the s-plane, each of width $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$, all map to the *same* unit circle. **Step 3.** The strip defined by: $$-\dfrac{\pi}{T} \leq \omega \leq +\dfrac{\pi}{T}$$ is called the **primary strip**. Only this strip provides a *bijective* (one-to-one) mapping with the z-plane. **Step 4.** Analog frequencies above $\omega_s/2$ get "folded" back (aliased) into the primary strip, which is why the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem** mandates $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$. > **Result:** All information about analog frequencies is preserved *only* within the primary strip. Out-of-band frequencies are indistinguishable from in-band frequencies after sampling. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Mapping Architecture (Mermaid) The following diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of the s-to-z mapping process, including the regions and constants involved. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["S-PLANE
Continuous Domain
s = sigma + j omega"] --> B["Mapping Kernel
z = exp to the power sT"] B --> C["Z-PLANE
Discrete Domain
z = r e to the power j theta"] A1["LHP
sigma less than 0"] -.-> B A2["Imaginary Axis
sigma equals 0"] -.-> B A3["RHP
sigma greater than 0"] -.-> B B --> C1["Inside Unit Circle
mod z less than 1
STABLE"] B --> C2["On Unit Circle
mod z equals 1
MARGINAL"] B --> C3["Outside Unit Circle
mod z greater than 1
UNSTABLE"] D["Primary Strip
omega from -pi slash T
to plus pi slash T"] -.-> B E["Sampling Period T
seconds"] --> B F["Nyquist Frequency
omega s over 2"] --> D style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style C1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style C2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825 style C3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 ``` ### 4.2 Stability Region Mapping Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Continuous Pole
s = -a + j b
a greater than 0, b nonzero"] --> M1["Apply z = exp sT"] M1 --> Z1["Discrete Pole
z = exp to the power -aT
e to the power j bT"] Z1 --> R1["Magnitude mod z equals exp -aT less than 1"] R1 --> DEC["DECAYS to zero
STABLE Response"] S2["Continuous Pole
s = plus j b
Imaginary Axis Only"] --> M2["Apply z = exp sT"] M2 --> Z2["Discrete Pole
z = e to the power j bT"] Z2 --> R2["Magnitude mod z equals 1"] R2 --> OSC["Sustained OSCILLATION
MARGINALLY STABLE"] S3["Continuous Pole
s = plus a + j b
a greater than 0"] --> M3["Apply z = exp sT"] M3 --> Z3["Discrete Pole
z = exp to the power plus aT
e to the power j bT"] Z3 --> R3["Magnitude mod z equals exp plus aT greater than 1"] R3 --> EXP["GROWS without bound
UNSTABLE Response"] style DEC fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style OSC fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style EXP fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c ``` ### 4.3 Constant-$\zeta$ and Constant-$\omega_n$ Locus Map ```mermaid graph TD SP["S-Plane Coordinate
sigma = -zeta omega_n
omega = omega n sqrt of 1 minus zeta squared"] SP --> CP1["Constant zeta line
zeta fixed, omega n varies"] SP --> CP2["Constant omega n line
omega n fixed, zeta varies"] CP1 --> MZ1["Maps to
LOGARITHMIC SPIRAL
in z-plane"] CP2 --> MZ2["Maps to
RADIAL RAY
from origin in z-plane"] MZ1 --> INT1["Spirals inward for LHP
originates at z equals 1"] MZ2 --> INT2["Ray angle equals omega n T
length equals exp to the power -zeta omega n T"] style SP fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style MZ1 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style MZ2 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** > The Mermaid diagrams above provide a *functional* and *architectural* representation of the s-to-z mapping. The logarithmic spirals and radial rays in the actual z-plane form a "spider-web" pattern centered around the origin, with the unit circle serving as the stability boundary. This pattern is the standard reference chart used in digital control textbooks for pole placement. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1: Conceptual Definition** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Define the mapping between the s-plane and z-plane. Why is the imaginary axis of the s-plane mapped to the unit circle of the z-plane? **Model Answer:** The mapping between the s-plane and z-plane is given by the fundamental relation: $$z = e^{sT}$$ where $s$ is the Laplace variable and $T$ is the sampling period. **Proof that the $j\omega$-axis maps to the unit circle:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ (purely imaginary, i.e., $\sigma = 0$): $$z = e^{j\omega T} = \cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)$$ Taking the modulus: $$\vert z \vert = \sqrt{\cos^2(\omega T) + \sin^2(\omega T)} = 1$$ Hence, every point on the imaginary axis of the s-plane maps to a point on the unit circle in the z-plane. Since the imaginary axis is the stability boundary in the s-domain, its image (the unit circle) becomes the **stability boundary in the z-domain**. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for proof)** --- #### **Question 2: Short Derivation** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** State and briefly justify the relationship between the s-plane variable $s$ and the z-plane variable $z$ for sampled-data systems. What is the significance of the primary strip? **Model Answer:** The relationship is: $$z = e^{sT} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad s = \frac{1}{T}\ln(z)$$ **Justification:** When a continuous signal $x(t)$ is sampled by an ideal impulse train, the resulting sampled signal is $x^*(t) = \sum x(kT)\delta(t - kT)$. The Z-transform of this sampled signal reduces exactly to evaluating $e^{sT}$ at the poles, leading to the direct substitution. **Significance of the Primary Strip:** The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ is **periodic** in $\omega$ with period $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$. Therefore, infinite horizontal strips of width $\omega_s$ in the s-plane all map to the same z-plane. The **primary strip**, defined by: $$-\frac{\pi}{T} \leq \omega \leq +\frac{\pi}{T}$$ provides a **bijective (one-to-one)** correspondence, ensuring that every point in the primary strip maps to a unique point in the z-plane, and vice versa. **(3 marks: 1 for statement + 1 for justification + 1 for primary strip significance)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 - Similar Model]` **Q (a)** Derive the relationship $z = e^{sT}$ between the s-plane and z-plane variables. Hence, show that the left half of the s-plane maps to the interior of the unit circle in the z-plane. **(7 marks)** **Q (b)** For a digital control system with sampling period $T = 0.5$ s, a continuous-time closed-loop pole is located at $s = -2 + j4$. Find the corresponding z-plane pole location. Comment on the stability. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question A (a):** **Step 1 — Sampling representation:** A continuous signal $x(t)$ sampled by an ideal impulse modulator yields: $$x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$$ **Step 2 — Laplace transform of the sampled signal:** $$X^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,e^{-skT}$$ **Step 3 — Z-transform definition:** $$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,z^{-k}$$ **Step 4 — Equating the two expressions:** Comparing the two summations, the substitution $z = e^{sT}$ makes the Laplace transform of the sampled signal equal to the Z-transform of the discrete sequence. Hence: $$z = e^{sT}$$ **Step 5 — LHP mapping:** Let $s = \sigma + j\omega$ with $\sigma < 0$ (LHP). Then: $$z = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T} = e^{\sigma T}\bigl[\cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)\bigr]$$ **Step 6 — Modulus calculation:** $$\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T} \cdot 1 = e^{\sigma T}$$ **Step 7 — Conclusion:** Since $\sigma < 0$, we have $\sigma T < 0$, hence $e^{\sigma T} < 1$, which means $\vert z \vert < 1$. Therefore, every LHP point lies strictly **inside the unit circle**. ∎ --- ### **Solution to Question A (b):** **Given:** $T = 0.5$ s, $s = -2 + j4$. **Step 1 — Apply the mapping formula:** $$z = e^{sT} = e^{(-2 + j4)(0.5)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the exponent:** $$sT = -1 + j2$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential:** $$z = e^{-1 + j2} = e^{-1} \cdot e^{j2}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the modulus:** $$\vert z \vert = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$ **Step 5 — Compute the angle:** $$\angle z = 2 \text{ radians} \approx 114.59^\circ$$ **Step 6 — Express in rectangular form:** $$z = 0.3679\cos(2) + j\,0.3679\sin(2) \approx -0.1478 + j\,0.3362$$ **Step 7 — Stability comment:** Since $\vert z \vert = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679 < 1$, the discrete pole lies **inside the unit circle**. Hence, the corresponding digital control system mode is **stable** and will decay over time. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Similar Model]` **Q (a)** Explain the concepts of (i) the primary strip and (ii) the mapping of constant damping ratio ($\zeta$) lines from the s-plane to the z-plane. Include a clear derivation. **(7 marks)** **Q (b)** A second-order continuous system has the closed-loop transfer function pole locations at $s_{1,2} = -1 \pm j\sqrt{3}$. If the system is sampled with $T = 1$ s, determine the z-plane pole locations and the discrete-time damping ratio $\zeta_d$. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question B (a):** **(i) Primary Strip:** The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ is **not one-to-one**. The argument of $z$ is $\omega T$, which is periodic modulo $2\pi$. Therefore, an infinite family of horizontal strips in the s-plane, each of vertical width $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$, all map to the *same* z-plane. The **primary strip** is the unique strip defined by: $$-\frac{\pi}{T} \leq \omega \leq +\frac{\pi}{T}$$ This strip has a **bijective** mapping with the z-plane, ensuring every analog frequency within it has a unique discrete counterpart. **(ii) Mapping of Constant $\zeta$ Lines:** A line of constant damping ratio $\zeta$ in the s-plane is defined by: $$s = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = \omega_n\left(-\zeta + j\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}\right)$$ where $\omega_n$ is the natural frequency (a variable). Applying the mapping: $$z = e^{sT} = \exp\!\left[\omega_n T\left(-\zeta + j\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}\right)\right]$$ Let $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$ so that $-\zeta = -\cos\phi$ and $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = \sin\phi$. Then: $$z = \exp\!\left[\omega_n T \cdot e^{j(\pi - \phi)}\right]$$ Since the argument of the exponent $(\pi - \phi)$ is **constant** for fixed $\zeta$, the locus is a **logarithmic spiral** in the z-plane, starting at $z = 1$ (when $\omega_n = 0$) and spiraling as $\omega_n$ increases. --- ### **Solution to Question B (b):** **Given:** $s_{1,2} = -1 \pm j\sqrt{3}$, $T = 1$ s. **Step 1 — Identify continuous-time parameters:** Comparing with $s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$: - $\sigma = -1 = -\zeta\omega_n$ - $\omega_d = \sqrt{3} = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ - $\vert s \vert = \omega_n = \sqrt{1 + 3} = 2$ rad/s - $\zeta = 1/\omega_n = 0.5$ **Step 2 — Apply the z-plane mapping:** $$z_{1,2} = e^{(-1 \pm j\sqrt{3})(1)} = e^{-1} \cdot e^{\pm j\sqrt{3}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute modulus and angle:** $$\vert z \vert = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679, \quad \angle z = \pm\sqrt{3} \text{ rad} \approx \pm 99.59^\circ$$ **Step 4 — Convert to rectangular form:** $$z_{1,2} = 0.3679\cos(\sqrt{3}) \pm j\,0.3679\sin(\sqrt{3})$$ $$z_{1,2} \approx -0.0583 \mp j\,0.3633$$ **Step 5 — Compute the discrete-time damping ratio:** Using the relation $\vert z \vert = e^{-\zeta\omega_n T} = e^{-0.5 \times 2 \times 1} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$. The discrete damping ratio is computed as: $$\zeta_d = -\ln(\vert z \vert)\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(\ln\vert z\vert)^2 + (\angle z)^2}}$$ $$\zeta_d = -\ln(0.3679)\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(1)^2 + (\sqrt{3})^2}} = (1)\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4}} = 0.5$$ **Step 6 — Stability:** Since $\vert z \vert \approx 0.3679 < 1$, both discrete poles lie **inside the unit circle**, confirming **stability**. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the sampling period $T$:** Many students write $z = e^s$ instead of $z = e^{sT}$. This single error can cost 2–3 marks. *Always* explicitly state and use $T$. > 2. **Confusing stability regions:** Students often write "LHP $\to$ outside unit circle" — this is the **inverse** and is wrong. Memorize: $\sigma < 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T} < 1 \Rightarrow$ **Inside** unit circle. > 3. **Ignoring the primary strip:** When asked about the "uniqueness" of the mapping, students often skip the periodic nature. Always mention that $z = e^{sT}$ is **periodic in $\omega$** with period $2\pi/T$. > 4. **Mixing $\omega$ and $\omega_d$:** The continuous-time damped frequency $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is *not* the same as $\omega_n$. Use the correct symbol. > 5. **Not commenting on stability in numerical problems:** KTU examiners explicitly look for the phrase "Since $\vert z \vert < 1$, the system is **stable**" at the end of numerical answers. Skipping this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > 6. **Aliasing oversight:** If a problem gives $\omega > \omega_s/2$, you **must** reduce it modulo $\omega_s$ into the primary strip *first*, *then* apply the mapping. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Core Equation:** $z = e^{sT}$ — the *only* formula you need as a starting point for all s-to-z problems. - **Modulus–Real Part Link:** $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ — the real part of $s$ controls the radial position in the z-plane. - **Angle–Imaginary Part Link:** $\angle z = \omega T$ — the imaginary part of $s$ controls the angular position. - **Stability Translation:** - LHP $\to$ Inside unit circle $\Rightarrow$ **Stable** - $j\omega$-axis $\to$ On unit circle $\Rightarrow$ **Marginally stable** - RHP $\to$ Outside unit circle $\Rightarrow$ **Unstable** - **Primary Strip:** Defined by $-\pi/T \leq \omega \leq \pi/T$; only this strip has a unique one-to-one mapping. - **Constant $\zeta$ Lines $\to$ Logarithmic Spirals** in the z-plane. - **Constant $\omega_n$ Lines $\to$ Radial Rays** in the z-plane. - **Nyquist Rate:** Sampling frequency must be at least twice the maximum signal frequency to avoid aliasing: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$. - **Sampling Period Range in KTU Problems:** $T = 0.1$ s to $T = 1.0$ s are most common in numericals. - **Critical Step for Numerical Stability Check:** Always compute $\vert z \vert$ and compare with $1$ — this is the **single most important KTU check**. - **Inverse Mapping Use Case:** $s = (1/T)\ln(z)$ is used in the **digital redesign of analog controllers** — a frequent KTU question. - **Phase Periodicity:** Because $\angle z = \omega T$ is periodic, an analog frequency $\omega$ and an analog frequency $\omega + 2\pi k/T$ are **indistinguishable** after sampling.

topics.title: Pulse Transfer Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT741 - DIGITAL CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pulse Transfer Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Pulse Transfer Function (PTF)** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) discrete-time system is formally defined as the **ratio of the Z-transform of the output sampled sequence $C(z)$ to the Z-transform of the input sampled sequence $R(z)$**, evaluated under the assumption of **zero initial conditions**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$G(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \quad \text{with} \quad \text{all initial conditions} = 0$$

In the frequency domain, the Z-transform serves as the discrete-time counterpart of the **Laplace transform**, and the Pulse Transfer Function serves as the discrete-time counterpart of the **Continuous Transfer Function** $G(s)$. It is the fundamental mathematical model used to describe the input-output relationship of **sampled-data (digital) control systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A Pulse Transfer Function exists **only for sampled-data systems**. For pure continuous systems, only the Laplace transfer function $G(s)$ exists. The PTF is defined for the relationship between *sampled* input and *sampled* output sequences.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **digital weighing scale** in a grocery store.

- You place an item (the **input** $r(t)$) on the scale.
- The scale takes a snapshot of the weight **only at fixed intervals** (say, every second) — this is the **sampler**.
- Each snapshot produces a number in a digital log: $r(0), r(1), r(2), \dots$

The Pulse Transfer Function $G(z)$ is essentially the **mathematical "rulebook"** that tells you, *given the sequence of weights placed on the scale, what the sequence of measured output readings will be*. It is the digital fingerprint of how the system transforms one set of discrete numbers into another set of discrete numbers.

Without a sampler, there is no digital "rulebook" — there is only the continuous rulebook $G(s)$.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Sampled Signal** $r^*(t)$ | A continuous signal $r(t)$ observed only at instants $t = kT$ |
| **Sampling Period $T$** | The fixed time interval between two consecutive samples (in **seconds**) |
| **Star Notation** $X^*(s)$ | Represents the sampled (starred) version of a continuous signal $X(s)$ |
| **ZOH** | Zero-Order Hold — a device that holds the sampled value constant between samples |

> [!NOTE]
> The starred notation $X^*(s)$ indicates a *sampled* signal. The Z-transform of $x^*(t)$ is denoted $X(z)$ and is a function of the complex variable $z = e^{sT}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How the Pulse Transfer Function Arises

When a continuous signal $r(t)$ is passed through a sampler, it becomes an **impulse train** $r^*(t)$ described mathematically as:

$$r^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \, \delta(t - kT)$$

The logic flow for obtaining $G(z)$ for a sampled system is:

1. **Step 1:** The continuous input $r(t)$ enters the system and is **sampled** at intervals $T$, producing the impulse-modulated sequence $r^*(t)$.
2. **Step 2:** This sampled signal passes through the linear plant $G(s)$, producing a continuous output $c(t)$ that is the **convolution** of $r^*(t)$ with the impulse response $g(t)$.
3. **Step 3:** The output is again **sampled** at synchronized intervals $T$, producing $c^*(t)$.
4. **Step 4:** The Z-transform is applied to both sampled signals: $C(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{c^*(t)\}$ and $R(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{r^*(t)\}$.
5. **Step 5:** The PTF is the ratio $G(z) = C(z)/R(z)$ — independent of the specific input, fully characterizing the system.

### 2.2 The General Form: $G(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \, \mathcal{Z}\left\{\dfrac{G(s)}{s}\right\}$

In most practical digital control systems, a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** precedes the plant. The ZOH reconstructs a staircase approximation of the sampled signal. The combined transfer function of the ZOH and the plant $G_p(s)$ in the $s$-domain is:

$$G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} \cdot G_p(s)$$

Taking the Z-transform of this entire block:

$$G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} \cdot G_p(s)\right\} = (1 - z^{-1}) \, \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $1 - z^{-1}$?** Because $1 - e^{-sT}$ in the $s$-domain corresponds exactly to $1 - z^{-1}$ in the $z$-domain, since $z = e^{sT}$.

### 2.3 Pulse Transfer Functions of Cascaded Elements

A critical exam point is determining when two cascaded blocks can be combined into a single PTF. The rule depends on whether a **sampler exists between the two blocks**.

**Case A: Sampler present between $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$**

$$G(z) = G_1(z) \cdot G_2(z)$$

**Case B: No sampler between $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$**

$$G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s)\} = G_1 G_2(z) \quad \text{(NOT the same as } G_1(z) G_2(z)\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often wrongly write $G(z) = G_1(z) G_2(z)$ when no sampler exists between blocks. This is **incorrect**. The Z-transform of a product in $s$-domain is *not* the product of individual Z-transforms. This is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU ESE.

### 2.4 Closed-Loop Pulse Transfer Functions

For a sampled-data feedback system, the PTF depends on the **location of the sampler in the feedback path**.

**Configuration 1: Unity Feedback (Sampler in Error Path)**

$$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

**Configuration 2: Sampler in Feedback Path Only**

$$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$

**Configuration 3: Sampler Both in Forward and Feedback Path**

$$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + GH(z)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the careful distinction: when the feedback signal is **not sampled**, the loop transfer function is $G(z)H(z)$ as a *product of two separate PTFs*, but when the feedback signal **is sampled**, the loop transfer function is $GH(z)$ (the Z-transform of the product).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Definition of PTF | $G(z) = C(z)/R(z)$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | ZOH + Plant PTF | $G(z) = (1-z^{-1}) \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)/s\}$ | Most common KTU form |
| 3 | Cascade with sampler | $G(z) = G_1(z) \cdot G_2(z)$ | Sampler between blocks |
| 4 | Cascade without sampler | $G(z) = G_1 G_2(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G_1(s) G_2(s)\}$ | NO simple factorization |
| 5 | ZOH factor in $z$ | $1 - z^{-1} = (z-1)/z$ | Used for simplification |
| 6 | Unity Feedback PTF | $C(z)/R(z) = G(z)/[1 + G(z)]$ | Sampler in error path |
| 7 | Sampled Feedback PTF | $C(z)/R(z) = G(z)/[1 + GH(z)]$ | Sampler in feedback |
| 8 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(z) = 0$ | Roots determine stability |
| 9 | Stability Region | $\vert z \vert < 1$ inside unit circle | All poles must lie inside |
| 10 | Bilinear Transform | $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ | Maps unit disk to left-half $w$-plane |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Pulse Transfer Function is the **backbone of every modern digital controller** — from the **flight control computer** in a Boeing 787 to the **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** in a car to the **cruise controller** in a Tesla. It allows engineers to:

- Design **digital PID controllers** with precise tuning.
- Predict whether a system will be **stable** before deployment.
- Implement control laws directly on **microcontrollers / DSPs / FPGAs** with predictable, repeatable behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: ZOH + Plant Pulse Transfer Function

**Problem Setup:** A Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) is cascaded with a plant $G_p(s)$. Derive the pulse transfer function $G(z) = C(z)/R(z)$ for the system.

**Step 1:** Write the input to the plant. The ZOH output is a staircase signal held for one sampling period $T$. The transfer function of an ideal ZOH is:

$$G_{h0}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

**Step 2:** The Laplace-domain output of the plant when driven by the ZOH is:

$$C(s) = \left[\frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}\right] \cdot G_p(s) \cdot R^*(s)$$

where $R^*(s)$ is the sampled (impulse-modulated) input.

**Step 3:** Expand the product:

$$C(s) = \left[\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right] R^*(s) - \left[e^{-sT} \cdot \frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right] R^*(s)$$

**Step 4:** Take the Z-transform of both sides. Recall the **shifting theorem**: $\mathcal{Z}\{e^{-sT} X(s)\} = z^{-1} X(z)$. Also, $\mathcal{Z}\{X(s) R^*(s)\} = X(z) R(z)$ for any $X(s)$.

$$C(z) = \left(1 - z^{-1}\right) \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \cdot R(z)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the PTF:

$$\boxed{\,G(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \left(1 - z^{-1}\right) \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}\,}$$

This is the **master formula** for any system with a ZOH followed by a plant.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Loop PTF (Sampler in Error Path)

**Problem Setup:** A sampler is placed at the input of the forward path, the plant is $G(s)$, and there is unity negative feedback. Derive $C(z)/R(z)$.

**Step 1:** The error signal after the comparator is $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$. The sampled error is:

$$E^*(s) = R^*(s) - C^*(s)$$

**Step 2:** The output (continuous) is:

$$C(s) = G(s) E^*(s)$$

**Step 3:** Sample both sides. Since $G(s)$ is a continuous transfer function and $E^*(s)$ is already a sampled signal, we have:

$$C^*(s) = [G(s) E^*(s)]^* = G^*(s) E^*(s)$$

This uses the property that **sampling the output of a linear system driven by a sampled input is equivalent to multiplying the Z-transforms**: $C(z) = G(z) E(z)$.

**Step 4:** Substitute $E(z) = R(z) - C(z)$:

$$C(z) = G(z) [R(z) - C(z)]$$

**Step 5:** Expand and collect terms:

$$C(z) = G(z) R(z) - G(z) C(z)$$

$$C(z) [1 + G(z)] = G(z) R(z)$$

$$\boxed{\,\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)}\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Closed-Loop PTF (Sampler in Feedback Path)

**Problem Setup:** Forward path has plant $G(s)$ with **no** sampler at its input. Feedback path has $H(s)$ with a sampler after it.

**Step 1:** Continuous output of the plant:

$$C(s) = G(s) E(s) = G(s) [R(s) - B(s)]$$

**Step 2:** Sampled feedback signal $B^*(s) = [H(s) C(s)]^*$. After sampling and Z-transforming the loop:

$$B(z) = GH(z) \, C(z)$$

**Step 3:** Sample the output (assuming an output sampler):

$$C^*(s) = [G(s) R(s) - G(s) B(s)]^*$$

This is **not** simply $G(z) R(z) - G(z) B(z)$ because $G(s)$ and $R(s)$ are not separated by a sampler. We need the Z-transform of the entire expression:

$$C(z) = GR(z) - GB(z)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $B(z) = GH(z) C(z)$:

$$C(z) = GR(z) - G \cdot GH(z) \, C(z) = GR(z) - G \cdot GH(z) \, C(z)$$

Wait — careful here. The product $G(s) R(s)$ has no sampler separating it, so we write $GR(z)$. The product $G(s) B(s)$ also has no sampler, but $B(s)$ itself is sampled, so $B(s) = B^*(s)$ and $G(s) B^*(s) = G(z) B(z)$.

**Step 5:** Re-derive cleanly:

$$C(z) = GR(z) - G(z) B(z) = GR(z) - G(z) \cdot GH(z) \cdot C(z)$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange:

$$C(z) [1 + G(z) GH(z)] = GR(z)$$

$$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{GR(z)}{1 + G(z) \cdot GH(z)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The numerator is $GR(z)$ (a *joint* Z-transform, NOT $G(z)R(z)$), and the denominator mixes $G(z)$ with the joint $GH(z)$. Memorize this asymmetric form — it is a KTU favourite.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


```mermaid
    A[Continuous Input r of t] --> B[Impulse Sampler at T]

    C --> D[Z Transform applied]
    D --> E[R of z]
```

### 4.3 Architecture Flow: Three Closed-Loop Configurations

```mermaid
    CFG1[Config 1: Sampler in Error Path] --> RES1[Cz over Rz equals Gz over 1 plus Gz]
    CFG2[Config 2: Sampler in Feedback Path] --> RES2[Cz over Rz equals Gz over 1 plus GHz]
    CFG3[Config 3: Sampler in Forward and Feedback] --> RES3[Cz over Rz equals Gz over 1 plus GHz]
```

> The **green path** (with samplers) yields a simple product $G_1(z) \cdot G_2(z)$, while the **red path** (without sampler) requires the joint Z-transform $G_1 G_2(z)$. Always verify sampler placement before combining blocks.
