# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State the necessary and sufficient condition for pole placement by state feedback in a discrete-time system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The system $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B})$ must be **completely controllable**, i.e., the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B} \; \mathbf{AB} \; \mathbf{A}^{2}\mathbf{B} \; \cdots \; \mathbf{A}^{n-1}\mathbf{B}]$ must have full rank $n$. If controllable, there exists a unique state feedback gain $\mathbf{K}$ that places the closed-loop poles at any desired (complex conjugate-symmetric) location in the z-plane, provided these locations are within the unit disk for stability.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What is a deadbeat controller? Why is it rarely used in noisy practical systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **deadbeat controller** places all closed-loop poles at the origin ($z = 0$), giving finite settling in at most $n$ sampling instants. While this is the **fastest possible** response, it requires very high control effort (large $\mathbf{K}$ gains), which **amplifies sensor noise** and may saturate actuators. It also demands precise plant model knowledge; any modeling error causes large overshoots. Hence, in noisy environments, poles are typically placed *near* the origin but not exactly at it.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to verify that $\mathcal{C}$ is **non-singular** before applying Ackermann's formula.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Sign error in $\alpha_c(\mathbf{A})$ — coefficients of $\alpha_c(z)$ must be used **with their signs as in the polynomial** (e.g., if $\alpha_c(z) = z^2 - z + 0.25$, then $\alpha_c(\mathbf{A}) = \mathbf{A}^2 - \mathbf{A} + 0.25\mathbf{I}$).
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** For observer design, students transpose the formula wrongly. Remember: for **controller** the row vector $\mathbf{e}_n^{\top}$ is on the **left**; for **observer** it is on the **right**.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Not specifying whether the desired poles are in the stable region ($\vert z \vert < 1$).
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–2 marks):** Failing to verify the final design by computing eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{BK}$ or $\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{LC}$.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Controller Design Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

Consider the discrete-time system:
$$\mathbf{x}(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.16 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{u}(k)$$

**(a)** Check the controllability of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a state feedback controller using Ackermann's formula to place the closed-loop poles at $z_1 = 0.5 + j0.5$ and $z_2 = 0.5 - j0.5$. Verify your result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1:** Form the controllability matrix:
$$\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B} & \mathbf{A}\mathbf{B} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -0.16 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute determinant:
$$\det(\mathcal{C}) = (0)(-0.16) - (1)(1) = -1 \neq 0$$

**Step 3:** Rank:
$$\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = 2 = n$$

**Conclusion:** System is **completely controllable**. $\checkmark$

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1:** Desired characteristic polynomial:
$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha_c(z) &= (z - 0.5 - j0.5)(z - 0.5 + j0.5) \\
&= (z-0.5)^2 + 0.25 \\
&= z^2 - z + 0.25 + 0.25 \\
&= z^2 - z + 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\alpha_c(\mathbf{A})$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}^2 &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.16 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.16 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.16 & 1 \\ -0.16 & 0.84 \end{bmatrix} \\
\alpha_c(\mathbf{A}) &= \mathbf{A}^2 - \mathbf{A} + 0.5\mathbf{I} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -0.16 & 1 \\ -0.16 & 0.84 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.16 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.34 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.34 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$:
$$\mathcal{C}^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1}\begin{bmatrix} -0.16 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.16 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Ackermann:
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.16 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.34 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.34 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.34 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.34 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.34 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Verification:**
$$\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{BK} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.16 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.34 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Characteristic polynomial: $\det(z\mathbf{I} - (\mathbf{A-BK})) = (z)(z-1) - (-0.5)(1) = z^2 - z + 0.5$. $\checkmark$ Matches $\alpha_c(z)$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Observer Design Focus

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

Consider:
$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** Design a full-order deadbeat observer using Ackermann's formula. Compute the observer gain $\mathbf{L}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the state feedback gain is $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$, derive the transfer function of the combined controller-observer compensator $G_c(z) = -\mathbf{K}(z\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} + \mathbf{BK} + \mathbf{LC})^{-1}\mathbf{L}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1:** Observability matrix:
$$\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{C} \\ \mathbf{CA} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det = -1 \neq 0 \Rightarrow \text{Observable.}$$

**Step 2:** Deadbeat polynomial: $\alpha_o(z) = z^2$, hence $\alpha_o(\mathbf{A}) = \mathbf{A}^2$.
$$\mathbf{A}^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.5 \\ 1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** $\mathcal{O}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (since it's its own negative swap).

**Step 4:** Apply observer Ackermann:
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{L} &= \alpha_o(\mathbf{A}) \, \mathcal{O}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.5 \\ 1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.5 \\ 1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Verify $\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{LC}$:
$$\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{LC} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues: $\det(z\mathbf{I} - (\mathbf{A-LC})) = z^2$. $\checkmark$ Both poles at origin.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1:** Compute $\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{BK} - \mathbf{LC}$ (interior of inverse):
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{BK} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{LC} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{BK} - \mathbf{LC} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Form $z\mathbf{I} - (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{BK} - \mathbf{LC})$:
$$M(z) = \begin{bmatrix} z + 0.5 & -1 \\ -1 & z + 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Invert and compute $G_c(z)$:
$$M(z)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(z+0.5)(z+1) - 1}\begin{bmatrix} z+1 & 1 \\ 1 & z+0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5z - 0.5}\begin{bmatrix} z+1 & 1 \\ 1 & z+0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Final compensator:
$$\begin{aligned}
G_c(z) &= -\mathbf{K} \, M(z)^{-1} \mathbf{L} \\
&= -\begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5z - 0.5} \begin{bmatrix} z+1 & 1 \\ 1 & z+0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= -\frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5z - 0.5} \begin{bmatrix} 1.5(z+1) - 1 & 1.5 - (z+0.5) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= -\frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5z - 0.5} \bigl(0.5(1.5z + 0.5) + (1 - z)\bigr) \\
&= -\frac{0.75z + 0.25 + 1 - z}{z^2 + 1.5z - 0.5} \\
&= -\frac{-0.25z + 1.25}{z^2 + 1.5z - 0.5} = \frac{0.25z - 1.25}{z^2 + 1.5z - 0.5}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\displaystyle G_c(z) = \frac{0.25(z - 5)}{z^2 + 1.5z - 0.5}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Pole Placement & Observer Design**

- ✅ **Pole placement requires controllability**; **observer design requires observability**. Always verify rank conditions first (1 mark reserved in KTU).
- ✅ **Ackermann's formula** for controller: $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{e}_n^{\top} \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_c(\mathbf{A})$ — note $\mathbf{e}_n^{\top}$ is on the **left**.
- ✅ **Ackermann's formula** for observer: $\mathbf{L} = \alpha_o(\mathbf{A}) \mathcal{O}^{-1} \mathbf{e}_n$ — note $\mathbf{e}_n$ is on the **right**.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** $\alpha_c(z) = z^n + \alpha_{n-1}z^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_0$. Substitute coefficients **as-is** into $\alpha_c(\mathbf{A})$.
- ✅ **Deadbeat design:** $\alpha(z) = z^n$ — all poles at the origin. Fastest but noise-sensitive.
- ✅ **Separation principle:** controller and observer poles are designed **independently** — total closed-loop order is $2n$.
- ✅ **Stability condition:** All desired poles must lie **inside the unit disk** ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$).
- ✅ **Compensator TF:** $G_c(z) = -\mathbf{K}(z\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} + \mathbf{BK} + \mathbf{LC})^{-1}\mathbf{L}$ — order $n$ digital filter.
- ✅ **Verification step:** Always recompute eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A-BK}$ and $\mathbf{A-LC}$ to confirm pole placement.
- ✅ **Estimator error dynamics:** $\mathbf{e}(k+1) = (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{LC})\mathbf{e}(k)$ — must be **asymptotically stable** ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$).
- ✅ **Reduced-order observer:** Dimension is $(n-p)$ where $p$ = number of independent measurements; only estimates the unmeasured part.
- ✅ **Multi-input systems:** Ackermann's classical form only applies to SISO. MIMO systems require generalized Sylvester equations or optimization-based pole placement (LQR/place command in MATLAB).
- ✅ **Sampling period $T$ matters:** Deadbeat response time $\leq nT$; in continuous time this can be unrealistically fast.
- ✅ **Trade-off in observer speed:** Faster observer poles $\Rightarrow$ better tracking but higher noise sensitivity. A common rule: place observer poles 2–5× faster than controller poles, or at $\sim 0.5 \times$ controller radius.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controllability and Observability - State Feedback Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT741 - DIGITAL CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Controllability and Observability - State Feedback Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Controllability

**Formal KTU Definition (Continuous-Time):**
A linear time-invariant (LTI) continuous-time system described by the state-space model

$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$$

$$y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$$

is said to be **completely state controllable** if, for any initial state $x(0) = x_0$ and any desired final state $x(t_f) = x_f$, there exists a piecewise continuous control input $u(t)$ defined over the finite interval $[0, t_f]$ that transfers the system from $x_0$ to $x_f$.

For discrete-time systems, the analogous statement applies to the pair $(G, H)$ of the difference equation $x(k+1) = G x(k) + H u(k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kalman's Controllability Test:** The continuous-time LTI system $(A, B)$ is completely controllable **if and only if** the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C}_M$ has **full rank**, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = n$, where $n$ is the order of the system (number of states).

$$\mathcal{C}_M = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2 B & \cdots & A^{n-1} B \end{bmatrix}_{n \times (nm)}$$

For a single-input system ($m = 1$), $\mathcal{C}_M$ is square of dimension $n \times n$, and the condition reduces to $\det(\mathcal{C}_M) \neq 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Controllability vs. Reachability:** In continuous time, controllability and reachability are equivalent. In discrete time, they can differ; however, for KTU exam purposes, treat them as equivalent unless explicitly distinguished by the question.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy for Controllability

Imagine you are driving a car in a parking lot. The car has two control inputs: the steering wheel (turn left/right) and the accelerator/brake (move forward/backward). **Controllability** asks the question: *"Starting from any arbitrary position and orientation in the lot, can I maneuver the car to any other arbitrary position and orientation in finite time using only the available inputs?"* If yes, the car is controllable. If, for example, the parking lot is in a deep valley and the car has no reverse gear, then you cannot reach the top of a hill — the system is **uncontrollable**.

## 1.2 Observability

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A continuous-time LTI system $(A, B, C, D)$ is said to be **completely observable** if, for any unknown initial state $x(0) = x_0$, there exists a finite time $t_f > 0$ such that the knowledge of the output $y(t)$ and the input $u(t)$ over the interval $[0, t_f]$ is sufficient to uniquely determine $x_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kalman's Observability Test:** The system $(A, C)$ is completely observable **if and only if** the observability matrix $\mathcal{O}_M$ has **full rank**, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M) = n$.

$$\mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^2 \\ \vdots \\ CA^{n-1} \end{bmatrix}_{(np) \times n}$$

For a single-output system ($p = 1$), $\mathcal{O}_M$ is square and the condition becomes $\det(\mathcal{O}_M) \neq 0$.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy for Observability

Consider a sealed black box containing a moving object. You can only see a small window (the output sensor) and you may know what forces (inputs) are applied externally. **Observability** asks: *"From the limited view through the window and the record of applied forces, can I deduce the exact position, velocity, and internal state of the object at any moment?"* If the window reveals enough motion to reconstruct the hidden state, the system is observable.

## 1.3 State Feedback Design

**Formal KTU Definition:**
**State feedback design** is the technique of placing the closed-loop poles of a system at desired locations in the s-plane (continuous) or z-plane (discrete) by feeding back all state variables through a constant gain matrix $K$:

$$u(t) = r(t) - K x(t)$$

where $r(t)$ is the reference input and $K$ is the **state feedback gain matrix** of dimension $m \times n$.

The resulting closed-loop system matrix becomes:

$$A_{cl} = A - B K$$

Pole placement is achievable **if and only if** the pair $(A, B)$ is completely controllable.

### 1.3.1 Intuitive Analogy for State Feedback

Think of a thermostat controlling a room's temperature. Instead of just turning the heater ON/OFF based on the current temperature (simple output feedback), **state feedback** uses additional internal information: current room temperature, rate of temperature change, outdoor temperature, humidity, and so on. By combining all this information with carefully chosen weights (the matrix $K$), the controller can predict the future behavior and act proactively to achieve smoother, faster, and more accurate temperature regulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Note:** Not all pole locations are physically realizable. For continuous systems, poles must lie in the **left half-plane (LHP)**. For discrete systems, poles must lie **inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$ in the z-plane.

## 1.4 Concept Map: Module 4 Flow

| Concept | What It Answers | Required Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Controllability | Can we move the state anywhere using inputs? | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = n$ |
| Observability | Can we infer the state from outputs? | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M) = n$ |
| Pole Placement | Can we shape the closed-loop dynamics? | System is controllable |
| Observer Design | Can we estimate unmeasured states? | System is observable |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Controllability and Observability Matter

These two concepts, introduced by **Rudolf Kalman in 1960**, form the **structural foundation** of modern control theory. They separate the question of *what can be done* (achievable performance) from *what must be done* (sensing/actuation hardware).

> [!TIP]
> **Duality Principle:** The continuous-time system $(A, B, C)$ is controllable **if and only if** the dual system $(A^T, C^T, B^T)$ is observable. This is a powerful theorem that lets us derive observer design results directly from controller design results.

## 2.2 Detailed Conditions for Controllability

For a continuous-time LTI system $\dot{x} = A x + B u$ of order $n$:

**Step 1:** Construct the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C}_M$ with $n$ block columns:

$$\mathcal{C}_M = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2 B & \cdots & A^{n-1} B \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Check the rank:
- $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = n \;\Rightarrow\;$ **completely controllable**
- $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) < n \;\Rightarrow\;$ **not completely controllable**

**Step 3 (Alternative for single-input SISO):** Compute $\det(\mathcal{C}_M)$:
- $\det(\mathcal{C}_M) \neq 0 \;\Rightarrow\;$ controllable
- $\det(\mathcal{C}_M) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\;$ not controllable

### 2.2.1 Popov-Belevitch-Hautus (PBH) Test

An equivalent, often more insightful, controllability test is the **PBH test**:

> The pair $(A, B)$ is controllable **if and only if** $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A \quad B] = n$ for every eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $A$.

Geometrically, no eigenvector of $A$ is orthogonal to all columns of $B$.

### 2.2.2 Gilbert's Canonical Form (for SISO systems)

If $(A, B)$ is controllable, there exists a similarity transformation $T$ that brings the system to **controllable canonical form**:

$$A_c = T A T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_c = T B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $a_i$ are the coefficients of the characteristic polynomial $\det(sI - A) = s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0$.

## 2.3 Detailed Conditions for Observability

For a continuous-time LTI system with output $y = C x$:

**Step 1:** Construct the observability matrix $\mathcal{O}_M$ with $n$ block rows:

$$\mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^2 \\ \vdots \\ CA^{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Check the rank:
- $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M) = n \;\Rightarrow\;$ **completely observable**
- $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M) < n \;\Rightarrow\;$ **not completely observable**

### 2.3.1 PBH Observability Test

The pair $(A, C)$ is observable **if and only if** $\text{rank}\begin{bmatrix} \lambda I - A \\ C \end{bmatrix} = n$ for every eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $A$.

## 2.4 Pole Placement via State Feedback

Given a controllable system $(A, B)$ with desired closed-loop characteristic polynomial:

$$p_d(s) = s^n + \alpha_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_1 s + \alpha_0$$

we wish to find $K$ such that:

$$\det(sI - (A - BK)) = p_d(s)$$

### 2.4.1 Ackermann's Formula (SISO Systems)

For a single-input system, the state feedback gain is:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathcal{C}_M^{-1} \, p_d(A)$$

where $p_d(A) = A^n + \alpha_{n-1} A^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_1 A + \alpha_0 I$ is the desired characteristic polynomial evaluated at the matrix $A$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Ackermann's formula is **valid only when** $\mathcal{C}_M$ is invertible (i.e., the system is controllable). For MIMO systems, the formula generalizes using right-inverse of $\mathcal{C}_M$.

## 2.5 Luenberger Observer Design

When not all states are measurable, we construct an **observer** (state estimator) that reconstructs the state from input-output measurements.

### 2.5.1 Full-Order Observer Structure

The **full-order Luenberger observer** has dynamics:

$$\dot{\hat{x}}(t) = A \hat{x}(t) + B u(t) + L \left[ y(t) - C \hat{x}(t) \right]$$

$$= (A - L C) \hat{x}(t) + B u(t) + L y(t)$$

where $\hat{x}(t)$ is the estimated state and $L$ is the **observer gain matrix** of dimension $n \times p$.

### 2.5.2 Observer Error Dynamics

Defining the estimation error $e(t) = x(t) - \hat{x}(t)$:

$$\dot{e}(t) = (A - L C) e(t)$$

The error decays to zero if all eigenvalues of $(A - LC)$ have **negative real parts**. We choose $L$ via **pole placement** to make the error dynamics as fast as desired.

### 2.5.3 Ackermann's Formula for Observer Gain (SISO)

For a SISO observable system:

$$L = p_d(A) \, \mathcal{O}_M^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $p_d(A)$ is the **desired observer characteristic polynomial** evaluated at $A$, and $\mathcal{O}_M$ is the observability matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Separation Principle:** The closed-loop poles of a system with state feedback controller + observer equal the **union** of the controller poles and the observer poles. They can be designed **independently**. This is a powerful result that dramatically simplifies practical design.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Condition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Controllability Matrix | $\mathcal{C}_M = [B, AB, A^2B, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$ | $n \times nm$ matrix |
| 2 | Controllability Condition | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = n$ | Necessary and sufficient |
| 3 | Observability Matrix | $\mathcal{O}_M = [C^T, (CA)^T, \ldots, (CA^{n-1})^T]^T$ | $np \times n$ matrix |
| 4 | Observability Condition | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M) = n$ | Necessary and sufficient |
| 5 | PBH Controllability | $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A, B] = n \;\; \forall \lambda \in \sigma(A)$ | Equivalent test |
| 6 | PBH Observability | $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A; C] = n \;\; \forall \lambda \in \sigma(A)$ | Equivalent test |
| 7 | Closed-Loop Matrix | $A_{cl} = A - BK$ | Controller design |
| 8 | Observer Error Matrix | $A_e = A - LC$ | Observer design |
| 9 | Ackermann (Controller) | $K = e_n^T \mathcal{C}_M^{-1} p_d(A)$ | $e_n = [0 \ldots 0\, 1]^T$ |
| 10 | Ackermann (Observer) | $L = p_d(A) \mathcal{O}_M^{-1} e_n$ | $e_n = [0 \ldots 0\, 1]^T$ |
| 11 | Duality | $(A,B)$ ctrb $\iff$ $(A^T, B^T)$ obsv | Powerful symmetry |
| 12 | Separation Principle | Eigenvalues of $A-BK-LC$ = $\sigma(A-BK) \cup \sigma(A-LC)$ | Independent design |
| 13 | Gilbert Form | $A_c$ companion form, $B_c = [0,\ldots,0,1]^T$ | Direct design route |
| 14 | Stability Region (Discrete) | Desired poles: $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ | Inside unit circle |

## 2.7 Engineering Applications

| Application | Role of Controllability/Observability |
|---|---|
| **Aircraft Autopilot** | Verifying all flight modes (pitch, roll, yaw) are controllable and that sensors can estimate hidden states. |
| **Robotic Manipulators** | Determining if joint torques can achieve any desired end-effector trajectory (controllability) and if joint encoders can reconstruct link positions (observability). |
| **Power System Stabilizers** | Designing state feedback to dampen low-frequency electromechanical oscillations. |
| **Chemical Process Control** | Verifying that reactant concentrations and temperatures are observable from available sensors. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Sensor placement and actuator sizing rely on controllability/observability analysis. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Controllability via Cayley-Hamilton

**Claim:** The set $\{B, AB, A^2 B, \ldots, A^{n-1} B\}$ spans the reachable subspace, and higher powers $A^k B$ for $k \geq n$ are linear combinations of lower powers.

**Proof Sketch:**

Consider the input $u(\tau)$ applied over $[0, t_f]$. The state solution is:

$$x(t_f) = e^{A t_f} x(0) + \int_0^{t_f} e^{A (t_f - \tau)} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{A t} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!}$ and the Cayley-Hamilton theorem (every matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation), we get:

$$e^{A t} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \alpha_k(t) A^k$$

Substituting this into the integral:

$$\int_0^{t_f} e^{A(t_f-\tau)} B u(\tau) d\tau = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} A^k B \underbrace{\int_0^{t_f} \alpha_k(t_f - \tau) u(\tau) d\tau}_{\beta_k}$$

This shows that the reachable set from $x(0) = 0$ is contained in the **column space** of $\mathcal{C}_M$. Conversely, if the column space of $\mathcal{C}_M$ is the entire $\mathbb{R}^n$, then any target state is reachable. Hence **controllability $\iff$ $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = n$**.

## 3.2 Detailed Pole Placement Example (SISO)

**Problem:** Given the controllable system

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

design a state feedback gain $K = [k_1 \; k_2]$ such that the closed-loop poles are at $s = -1 \pm j$ (i.e., $\zeta \approx 0.707$, $\omega_n = \sqrt{2}$).

### Step 1: Verify Controllability

$$\mathcal{C}_M = [B, AB] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(\mathcal{C}_M) = (0)(-3) - (1)(1) = -1 \neq 0$$

So the system is **controllable** — pole placement is possible. $\mathcal{C}_M$ is invertible.

### Step 2: Compute $\mathcal{C}_M^{-1}$

$$\mathcal{C}_M^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1} \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Form the Desired Characteristic Polynomial

The desired poles are $s_1, s_2 = -1 \pm j$, so:

$$p_d(s) = (s - (-1+j))(s - (-1-j)) = (s+1)^2 + 1 = s^2 + 2s + 2$$

Hence: $\alpha_1 = 2, \alpha_0 = 2$.

### Step 4: Compute $p_d(A) = A^2 + 2A + 2I$

First, compute $A^2$:

$$A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (0)(0)+(1)(-2) & (0)(1)+(1)(-3) \\ (-2)(0)+(-3)(-2) & (-2)(1)+(-3)(-3) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -3 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now compute $2A$:

$$2A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ -4 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$$

And $2I$:

$$2I = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Add them:

$$p_d(A) = A^2 + 2A + 2I = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -3 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ -4 & -6 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Apply Ackermann's Formula

With $e_2^T = [0, 1]$:

$$K = e_2^T \mathcal{C}_M^{-1} p_d(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

First multiply the last two:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (3)(0)+(1)(2) & (3)(-1)+(1)(3) \\ (1)(0)+(0)(2) & (1)(-1)+(0)(3) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now multiply with $e_2^T$:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 6: Verification

$$A - BK = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Check characteristic polynomial:

$$\det(sI - (A-BK)) = \det\begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+2 \end{bmatrix} = s(s+2) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 2s + 2 \;\checkmark$$

The desired polynomial is obtained.

## 3.3 Detailed Observer Design Example (SISO)

**Problem:** For the system in §3.2 with $C = [1 \; 0]$ (only position is measured), design a full-order Luenberger observer with error dynamics poles at $s = -5, -5$ (5× faster than the controller).

### Step 1: Verify Observability

$$\mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_2$$

So $\det(\mathcal{O}_M) = 1 \neq 0$ — the system is **observable**. $\mathcal{O}_M^{-1} = I_2$.

### Step 2: Desired Observer Characteristic Polynomial

$$p_{obs}(s) = (s+5)^2 = s^2 + 10s + 25$$

So $\alpha_1 = 10, \alpha_0 = 25$.

### Step 3: Compute $p_{obs}(A) = A^2 + 10A + 25I$

$$10A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 \\ -20 & -30 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 25I = \begin{bmatrix} 25 & 0 \\ 0 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$p_{obs}(A) = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -3 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 \\ -20 & -30 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 25 & 0 \\ 0 & 25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 23 & 7 \\ -14 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Apply Observer Ackermann's Formula

$$L = p_{obs}(A) \, \mathcal{O}_M^{-1} \, e_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 23 & 7 \\ -14 & 2 \end{bmatrix} I_2 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Verify Observer Poles

$$A - L C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0-7 & 1-0 \\ -2-2 & -3-0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -7 & 1 \\ -4 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(sI - (A-LC)) = \det\begin{bmatrix} s+7 & -1 \\ 4 & s+3 \end{bmatrix} = (s+7)(s+3) + 4 = s^2 + 10s + 25 \;\checkmark$$

The estimation error decays with time constant $\approx 0.1$ s, 5× faster than the controlled system.

## 3.4 Numerical: PBH Test Illustration

**Problem:** Test controllability of the system

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Eigenvalues:** $\lambda_1 = 1, \lambda_2 = -1$.

**PBH Test for $\lambda = 1$:**

$$[\lambda_1 I - A \quad B] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row-reduce: $\text{rank} = 2 = n$. OK so far.

**PBH Test for $\lambda = -1$:**

$$[\lambda_2 I - A \quad B] = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row-reduce: only one non-zero row. $\text{rank} = 1 < n = 2$.

**Conclusion:** The system is **NOT controllable** — the second mode ($x_2$) cannot be influenced by $u$.

**Cross-check with Kalman test:**

$$\mathcal{C}_M = [B, AB] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = 1 < 2 \;\checkmark$$

## 3.5 Analytical Summary: Steps to Verify a KTU 4-mark Question

| Step | Action | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Write down $A, B, C, n$ | 0.5 |
| 2 | Form $\mathcal{C}_M$ or $\mathcal{O}_M$ with $n$ blocks | 1 |
| 3 | Compute rank or determinant | 1 |
| 4 | State conclusion with justification | 1.5 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refR["Reference r(t)"] --> sumNode1(("+"))
    sumNode1 --> ctrlK["Controller Gain K"]
    ctrlK --> sumNode2(("+"))
    sumNode2 --> plantA["Plant A, B"]
    plantA --> stateX["State x(t)"]
    stateX --> sumNode1
    stateX --> outY["Output y(t) = Cx"]
    plantA -. feedback path .-> ctrlK

    classDef ref fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000

    class refR ref
    class ctrlK ctrl
    class plantA plant
    class stateX,outY sig
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture:**

- **Reference input $r(t)$** enters the summing junction.
- **Summing junction** computes $r(t) - Kx(t)$ to produce control signal $u(t)$.
- **Plant dynamics** $(A, B)$ integrate the control into state evolution $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$.
- **Output equation** $y = Cx$ provides measurement signal.
- **State feedback** $Kx(t)$ closes the loop; $K$ is designed via pole placement.

## 4.2 Luenberger Observer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inU["Input u(t)"] --> plantReal["Plant A, B, C"]
    inU --> observerCopy["Observer Copy A, B"]
    plantReal --> stateX["True State x(t)"]
    plantReal --> outY["Output y(t)"]
    outY --> sumObs(("+"))
    obsOut["Estimated Output C xhat"] --> sumObs
    sumObs --> gainL["Observer Gain L"]
    gainL --> observerCopy
    observerCopy --> stateXhat["Estimated State xhat(t)"]
    stateXhat --> obsOut
    stateX -. comparison .-> errorE["Error e = x - xhat"]

    classDef sig fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef obs fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000

    class inU,outY,stateX,errorE,obsOut,stateXhat sig
    class observerCopy,gainL obs
    class plantReal plant
```

## 4.3 Combined Controller + Observer (Separation Principle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refR2["r(t)"] --> sumC2(("+"))
    sumC2 --> ctrlK2["Controller K"]
    ctrlK2 --> inU2["u(t)"]
    inU2 --> plantReal2["Plant A, B, C"]
    plantReal2 --> stateX2["x(t)"]
    plantReal2 --> outY2["y(t)"]
    stateX2 --> sumC2
    inU2 --> observer2["Observer A-LC, B, L"]
    outY2 --> observer2
    observer2 --> stateXhat2["xhat(t)"]
    stateXhat2 --> sumC2

    classDef ref fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000

    class refR2 ref
    class ctrlK2 ctrl
    class plantReal2 plant
    class inU2,outY2,stateX2,stateXhat2 sig
    class observer2 obs
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow: Test & Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Given system A, B, C"] --> chkCtrl["Check rank of CM = B AB ... An-1 B"]
    chkCtrl --> qCtr{"rank = n?"}
    qCtr -- Yes --> canPlace["Pole placement is possible"]
    qCtr -- No --> noPlace["Cannot place poles; redesign plant/sensors"]
    canPlace --> ackC["Compute K via Ackermann or transformation"]
    ackC --> verifyC["Verify det sI - A + BK = pd s"]

    startB["Given system A, B, C"] --> chkObs["Check rank of OM"]
    chkObs --> qObs{"rank = n?"}
    qObs -- Yes --> canObs["Observer design is possible"]
    qObs -- No --> noObs["Cannot design full-order observer"]
    canObs --> ackO["Compute L via Ackermann observer formula"]
    ackO --> verifyO["Verify det sI - A + LC = pobs s"]

    verifyC --> done["Design complete"]
    verifyO --> done

    classDef good fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000

    class canPlace,canObs,done good
    class noPlace,noObs bad
    class startA,startB,chkCtrl,chkObs,ackC,ackO,verifyC,verifyO step
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define controllability of an LTI system. State the Kalman test for controllability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A continuous-time LTI system $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ of order $n$ is said to be **completely state controllable** if for any initial state $x(0)$ and any desired final state $x(t_f)$, there exists an input $u(t)$ over $[0, t_f]$ that transfers the system from $x(0)$ to $x(t_f)$.

**Kalman's Test:** The system is completely controllable **if and only if** the controllability matrix

$$\mathcal{C}_M = [B \; AB \; A^2 B \; \cdots \; A^{n-1} B]$$

has full rank, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = n$. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for test statement + matrix)*

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the dual relationship between controllability and observability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **duality principle** states that the system $(A, B, C)$ is **controllable if and only if** the dual system $(A^T, C^T, B^T)$ is **observable**. Mathematically:

$$\text{rank}[\,B \; AB \; \cdots \; A^{n-1}B\,] = n \iff \text{rank}\begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ \vdots \\ CA^{n-1} \end{bmatrix} = n$$

This principle allows the design of observers from controller design theory and vice versa. *(1.5 marks for the principle, 1.5 marks for the mathematical statement)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Controller Design**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

Consider the LTI system:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Test the controllability and observability of the system. Comment on the feasibility of pole placement and observer design.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Design a state feedback gain $K = [k_1 \; k_2]$ to place the closed-loop poles at $s = -2 \pm j2$. Use Ackermann's formula.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Controllability Matrix:**

$$\mathcal{C}_M = [B \; AB] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(\mathcal{C}_M) = (0)(1) - (1)(1) = -1 \neq 0$$

Therefore, $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = 2 = n$. The system is **completely controllable** (1 mark).

**Observability Matrix:**

$$\mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(\mathcal{O}_M) = (1)(1) - (0)(1) = 1 \neq 0$$

Therefore, $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M) = 2 = n$. The system is **completely observable** (1 mark).

**Conclusion:** Since the system is both controllable and observable:
- Pole placement is **feasible** (1 mark).
- Full-order observer design is **feasible** (1 mark).
- The complete design (controller + observer) can be done (1 mark).
- The closed-loop poles equal the union of controller and observer poles by the separation principle (1 mark).
- Overall feasibility comment with reference to the separation principle (1 mark).

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Compute $\mathcal{C}_M^{-1}$:**

$$\mathcal{C}_M^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Desired Characteristic Polynomial:**

$$p_d(s) = (s+2-j2)(s+2+j2) = (s+2)^2 + 4 = s^2 + 4s + 8$$

So $\alpha_1 = 4, \alpha_0 = 8$.

**Step 3: Compute $p_d(A) = A^2 + 4A + 8I$:**

$$A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$4A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 8I = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$p_d(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 1+4+8 & 2+4+0 \\ 0+0+0 & 1+4+8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13 & 6 \\ 0 & 13 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Apply Ackermann's Formula** with $e_2^T = [0, 1]$:

$$K = [0 \; 1] \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 13 & 6 \\ 0 & 13 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiply the last two matrices:

$$\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 13 & 6 \\ 0 & 13 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -13+0 & -6+13 \\ 13+0 & 6+0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -13 & 7 \\ 13 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now multiply with $[0, 1]$:

$$K = [0 \; 1] \begin{bmatrix} -13 & 7 \\ 13 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Verification:**

$$A - BK = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}[13 \; 6] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -13 & -5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(sI - (A-BK)) = \det\begin{bmatrix} s-1 & -1 \\ 13 & s+5 \end{bmatrix} = (s-1)(s+5) + 13 = s^2 + 4s + 8 \;\checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{K = [13 \; 6]}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Observer Design**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

For the system in Question A, suppose only the output $y(t)$ is available for measurement. The velocity state $x_2$ is not directly measurable.

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Design a full-order Luenberger observer using Ackermann's formula such that the estimation error decays with characteristic polynomial $p_{obs}(s) = s^2 + 12s + 45$. Comment on the choice of observer poles.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Using the separation principle, write down the complete state-space equations of the closed-loop system (controller + observer) and the equation governing the estimation error dynamics. Justify why both designs can be performed independently.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Observability Matrix** (from Question A): $\mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathcal{O}_M^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2: Compute $p_{obs}(A) = A^2 + 12A + 45I$:**

We already have $A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

$$12A = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 12 \\ 0 & 12 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 45I = \begin{bmatrix} 45 & 0 \\ 0 & 45 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$p_{obs}(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 1+12+45 & 2+12+0 \\ 0+0+0 & 1+12+45 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 58 & 14 \\ 0 & 58 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Apply Observer Ackermann's Formula** with $e_2 = [0, 1]^T$:

$$L = p_{obs}(A) \, \mathcal{O}_M^{-1} e_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 58 & 14 \\ 0 & 58 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiply middle two:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now:

$$L = \begin{bmatrix} 58 & 14 \\ 0 & 58 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 58 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Verification:**

$$A - LC = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 58 \end{bmatrix} [1 \; 0] = \begin{bmatrix} 1-14 & 1-0 \\ 0-58 & 1-0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -13 & 1 \\ -58 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(sI - (A-LC)) = \det\begin{bmatrix} s+13 & -1 \\ 58 & s-1 \end{bmatrix} = (s+13)(s-1) + 58 = s^2 + 12s + 45 \;\checkmark$$

**Comment on Observer Pole Choice (3 marks of 7):**

The desired observer poles are at $s = -3, -15$ (roots of $s^2 + 12s + 45 = 0$). The **observer poles are chosen 3 to 5 times farther left in the s-plane** than the controller poles (which were at $s = -2 \pm j2$, with real part $-2$). This is because:
1. The estimator must converge much faster than the controlled response, so it does not become the bottleneck.
2. However, observer poles that are too far left make $L$ large, amplifying measurement noise — a practical trade-off.
3. The rule of thumb is: real part of observer poles $\geq 3 \times$ real part of controller poles.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{L = [14 \; 58]^T}$

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**State Feedback Control Law:**

$$u(t) = r(t) - K \hat{x}(t) = r(t) - [13 \; 6] \hat{x}(t)$$

**Plant Dynamics:**

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} x(t) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(t)$$

**Observer Dynamics:**

$$\dot{\hat{x}}(t) = (A - LC)\hat{x}(t) + Bu(t) + Ly(t)$$

$$\dot{\hat{x}}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} -13 & 1 \\ -58 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \hat{x}(t) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(t) + \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 58 \end{bmatrix} y(t)$$

**Error Dynamics:** With $e(t) = x(t) - \hat{x}(t)$:

$$\dot{e}(t) = (A - LC) e(t) = \begin{bmatrix} -13 & 1 \\ -58 & 1 \end{bmatrix} e(t)$$

**Closed-Loop Composite System (Augmented Form):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{e} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A - BK & BK \\ 0 & A - LC \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ e \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} B \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} r$$

**Separation Principle Justification (3 marks of 7):**

The block triangular structure of the augmented system matrix shows that:

$$\sigma(A_{cl}) = \sigma(A - BK) \cup \sigma(A - LC)$$

That is, the closed-loop poles are precisely the union of controller poles and observer poles, with **no coupling** between them. This is because the upper-left block $(A - BK)$ determines how the state evolves, and the lower-right block $(A - LC)$ determines how the error evolves — and the off-diagonal block $BK$ only couples $e$ to $x$ (not vice versa). 

Therefore:
- $K$ can be designed using pole placement on $(A, B)$ alone.
- $L$ can be designed using pole placement on $(A^T, C^T)$ (the dual system) alone.
- Both designs proceed **independently**, then are combined.

This is the **separation principle**, and it is one of the cornerstones of modern control theory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:**
> Many students write $u(t) = r(t) - Kx(t)$ even when an observer is used. **The control law MUST use the estimated state $\hat{x}(t)$, not the true state $x(t)$** — because $x(t)$ is not fully measured! Writing $u = r - Kx$ instead of $u = r - K\hat{x}$ will cost **2 marks** outright.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #2:**
> When asked to "design an observer", students sometimes compute the **controller gain** $K$ instead of the **observer gain** $L$. The Ackermann formulas look similar, but the positions of the controllability/observability matrix and the unit vector $e_n$ are **swapped**! In the observer formula, $p_d(A)$ is on the **left** of $\mathcal{O}_M^{-1}$, not the right.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #3:**
> Failing to **verify** the answer. After finding $K$ or $L$, ALWAYS compute $\det(sI - A + BK)$ or $\det(sI - A + LC)$ and show it equals the desired polynomial. KTU examiners explicitly look for this verification step. Omitting it loses 1-2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (rapid recall before exam):**

- [ ] **Controllability matrix** has $n$ block columns: $[B, AB, A^2B, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$ — $n$ blocks, not $n+1$.
- [ ] **Observability matrix** has $n$ block rows: $[C; CA; \ldots; CA^{n-1}]$ — $n$ blocks.
- [ ] **Controllability $\Rightarrow$ Pole placement possible** (necessary AND sufficient).
- [ ] **Observability $\Rightarrow$ Observer design possible** (necessary AND sufficient).
- [ ] For SISO, the matrix is square — use $\det \neq 0$ instead of rank.
- [ ] **Ackermann controller formula** has $p_d(A)$ on the **right** of $\mathcal{C}_M^{-1}$.
- [ ] **Ackermann observer formula** has $p_d(A)$ on the **left** of $\mathcal{O}_M^{-1}$.
- [ ] The unit vector $e_n$ is always $[0, 0, \ldots, 0, 1]^T$ with the 1 in the $n$-th position.
- [ ] **PBH test**: $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A, B] = n$ for **all** eigenvalues $\lambda$.
- [ ] **Duality**: $(A, B)$ controllable $\iff$ $(A^T, C^T)$ observable.
- [ ] **Stability regions**: LHP for continuous, $\vert z \vert < 1$ for discrete.
- [ ] **Observer poles are 3-5× faster** than controller poles (rule of thumb).
- [ ] **Separation principle**: closed-loop poles = controller poles $\cup$ observer poles.
- [ ] The control law uses $\hat{x}(t)$, NOT $x(t)$ when an observer is in the loop.
- [ ] Always **verify** the answer by computing the resulting characteristic polynomial.
- [ ] **Minimum marks to secure in KTU Module 4:** clearly state conditions (1 mark), compute matrix (1 mark), compute rank/det (1 mark), state conclusion (1 mark) — for any 4-mark question.
- [ ] **For 14-mark questions**, follow the 7+7 split: Part (a) tests/derives, Part (b) applies the result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the Two Key Matrices:**
> - **C**ontrollability → **C**olumns: $B, AB, A^2B, \ldots$ placed as **columns**.
> - **O**bservability → **O**ver rows: $C, CA, CA^2, \ldots$ placed as **rows**.
> The "columns of controllability" and "rows of observability" are easy to confuse under exam stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus Tip — KTU's Favorite Question Pattern:**
> The most commonly asked 14-mark question combines **(a) test controllability/observability** + **(b) design $K$ via Ackermann**. Master this pattern, and you have secured nearly 100% marks on Module 4 controller design questions. The observer-based 14-marker appears in roughly 30% of past papers and is worth special attention.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pole Placement by State Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT741 - DIGITAL CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pole Placement by State Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pole Placement by State Feedback** is a systematic closed-loop pole-assignment design methodology in modern control theory wherein the eigenvalues (closed-loop poles) of a controllable, linear, time-invariant (LTI) continuous-time or discrete-time system are arbitrarily relocated to any desired locations in the complex plane (subject to complex-conjugate pairing) by feeding back the entire state vector through a constant gain matrix $K$.

For a state-space system of the form:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$$

$$y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$$

the state feedback control law is expressed as:

$$u(t) = -Kx(t) + r(t)$$

where $r(t)$ is the reference input, and substituting this into the open-loop state equation yields the closed-loop dynamics:

$$\dot{x}(t) = (A - BK)x(t) + Br(t)$$

The design objective is to choose the feedback gain matrix $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ such that the eigenvalues of $(A-BK)$ exactly match a set of designer-specified poles $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Pole Placement problem assumes the system is **completely state controllable**. The classical Ackermann's formula and the direct coefficient comparison method are the two most tested approaches in KTU ESE questions for this topic.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Steering Wheel of a Car"**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car that is slightly misaligned — it pulls toward the left. You, as the driver, constantly apply small corrections to the steering wheel based on *multiple observations* — your speed, your angle with the road's edge, the rate at which you are drifting, and so on. Each of these "observations" is a **state variable**. The way you weigh each observation before applying a correction is essentially the **state feedback gain matrix $K$**.
>
> **Pole placement** is the engineering version of this: instead of guessing, you *mathematically compute* the exact weight (gain) for each state so that the car's response (the *closed-loop poles*) matches the *desired handling behavior* — fast but not jerky, stable, and smooth.

## 1.3 Why Pole Placement Matters in Engineering

- **Aircraft Autopilots:** Lateral and longitudinal dynamics are reshaped by gain-scheduled pole placement.
- **Robotic Manipulators:** Joint position and velocity states are weighted to achieve critical damping.
- **Hard Disk Drive Servos:** The head-positioning actuator's poles are placed for sub-millisecond settling.
- **DC Motor Speed Control:** The armature-current and shaft-speed states are combined to deliver critically damped or Bessel-style response.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Pole placement is *not* an optimization method. It is a *direct synthesis* method — you specify where you want the poles, and the design *guarantees* they end up there (provided the system is controllable).

## 1.4 Pole Placement in the Discrete-Time (Digital) Domain

For a digital control system, the discrete state-space model obtained via ZOH equivalent is:

$$x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$$

$$u(k) = -Kx(k)$$

Substituting yields the closed-loop matrix $A_{cl} = \Phi - \Gamma K$, whose eigenvalues must lie strictly **inside the unit circle** in the Z-plane for asymptotic stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Distinction:**
> - **Continuous-time:** Closed-loop poles must lie in the **left-half s-plane** ($\text{Re}(s_i) < 0$).
> - **Discrete-time:** Closed-loop poles must lie **inside the unit circle** ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$).
>
> Both domains share the *same design mathematics* for $K$; only the target pole locations differ.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — The "Why" Behind Pole Placement

The motivation stems from a key limitation of classical control: classical techniques (root locus, Bode, Nyquist) are restricted to **single-input, single-output (SISO)** systems and offer **indirect** design freedom. In contrast, modern state-space methods provide:

1. **Direct placement of poles** in the complex plane.
2. **Extension to multi-input, multi-output (MIMO)** systems.
3. **Full utilization of all internal state information** — not just the output.
4. **Analytical, closed-form computation** of the gain matrix (no iterative tuning required).

## 2.2 The Pole Placement Problem — Formal Statement

**Given:**
- A controllable pair $(A, B)$ with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$.
- A desired set of closed-loop poles $\{\lambda_1^d, \lambda_2^d, \dots, \lambda_n^d\}$.

**Find:** A constant gain matrix $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ such that:

$$\text{eig}(A - BK) = \{\lambda_1^d, \lambda_2^d, \dots, \lambda_n^d\}$$

## 2.3 Necessary and Sufficient Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Theorem of Pole Placement:**
>
> A state feedback law $u = -Kx$ can arbitrarily place all $n$ closed-loop poles of the pair $(A, B)$ at desired locations if and only if the pair $(A, B)$ is **completely state controllable** — that is, the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C}$ has full rank $n$:
>
> $$\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = \text{rank}\begin{bmatrix} B \mid AB \mid A^2B \mid \cdots \mid A^{n-1}B \end{bmatrix} = n$$

If the system is **uncontrollable**, certain poles (the uncontrollable modes) remain fixed and cannot be moved by state feedback.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Remarks / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-loop state equation | $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| 2 | State feedback law | $u = -Kx + r$ | $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ |
| 3 | Closed-loop system matrix | $A_{cl} = A - BK$ | $n \times n$ matrix |
| 4 | Controllability matrix | $\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2B & \cdots & A^{n-1}B \end{bmatrix}$ | Must have rank $n$ |
| 5 | Controllability condition | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = n$ | Necessary and sufficient for pole placement |
| 6 | Desired characteristic polynomial | $\alpha_d(s) = s^n + \alpha_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_1 s + \alpha_0 = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(s - \lambda_i^d)$ | $\lambda_i^d$ = desired pole |
| 7 | Open-loop characteristic polynomial | $\alpha(s) = \det(sI - A) = s^n + a_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0$ | Coefficients of $\det(sI-A)$ |
| 8 | Ackermann's formula | $K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_d(A)$ | $\alpha_d(A) = A^n + \alpha_{n-1}A^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_0 I$ |
| 9 | Discrete-time closed-loop matrix | $\Phi_{cl} = \Phi - \Gamma K$ | Poles must satisfy $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ |
| 10 | Stability (continuous) | $\text{Re}(\lambda_i^d) < 0 \;\; \forall i$ | Left-half s-plane |
| 11 | Stability (discrete) | $\vert z_i^d \vert < 1 \;\; \forall i$ | Inside unit circle |
| 12 | Characteristic equation | $\det(sI - A + BK) = 0$ | Roots are closed-loop poles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the bedrock of **observer design** (Luenberger, Kalman), **LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator)**, and **robust control** techniques. The KTU Board frequently tests Ackermann's formula as a 14-mark problem.

## 2.5 The Two Principal Design Methods

### Method 1: Direct Coefficient Comparison (Transform Approach)

This method transforms the system into **controllable canonical form (CCF)**, applies simple feedback, and then transforms $K$ back to the original coordinates.

**Steps (high level):**
1. Compute the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C}$ and verify $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = n$.
2. Construct the transformation matrix $T = \mathcal{C} \cdot W$, where $W$ contains the coefficients of the open-loop characteristic polynomial.
3. Transform: $A_c = T^{-1} A T$, $B_c = T^{-1} B$.
4. In CCF, the feedback gain is simply $K_c = \begin{bmatrix} a_0 - \alpha_0 & a_1 - \alpha_1 & \cdots & a_{n-1} - \alpha_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$ for SISO ($m=1$).
5. Transform back: $K = K_c T^{-1}$.

### Method 2: Ackermann's Formula (Most Tested in KTU)

A direct, one-shot formula for $K$ without requiring a transformation:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}_{1 \times n} \cdot \mathcal{C}^{-1} \cdot \alpha_d(A)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why Ackermann's is Preferred in KTU Exams:** It is a single closed-form expression. Students only need to (i) compute $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$, (ii) form $\alpha_d(A)$ by substituting $A$ into the desired characteristic polynomial, and (iii) multiply. The board examiner expects to see *all three sub-products* explicitly shown.

## 2.6 Handling Complex-Conjugate Pole Pairs

When the desired poles include complex-conjugate pairs (e.g., $-\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$), the coefficients of the desired characteristic polynomial **must be real**. The procedure remains identical — the matrix $K$ is computed as a real matrix because $\alpha_d(A)$ is a polynomial in $A$ with real coefficients.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop State Equation

We start with the open-loop system:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$$

The state feedback control law (with $r(t)=0$ for the homogeneous pole-placement problem) is:

$$u(t) = -Kx(t)$$

Substituting the control law into the open-loop state equation:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + B\bigl(-Kx(t)\bigr)$$

Distributing the terms:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) - BKx(t)$$

Factoring out $x(t)$:

$$\dot{x}(t) = (A - BK)x(t)$$

This is the **closed-loop homogeneous state equation**, and the closed-loop system matrix is:

$$A_{cl} = A - BK$$

The closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$\det(sI - A + BK) = 0$$

The roots of this equation are the **closed-loop poles** — the eigenvalues of $A_{cl}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Controllability Condition

Starting from the controllability condition for arbitrary pole placement, we argue by contradiction. Suppose a mode $\lambda_i$ of $A$ is uncontrollable. Then there exists a left eigenvector $v_i^T$ such that $v_i^T A = \lambda_i v_i^T$ and $v_i^T B = 0$. For any $K$:

$$v_i^T (A - BK) = \lambda_i v_i^T - v_i^T BK = \lambda_i v_i^T$$

This shows $\lambda_i$ remains an eigenvalue of $(A-BK)$ for *every* $K$, meaning the pole cannot be moved. Hence, controllability is necessary.

Conversely, if the system is controllable, the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C}$ is nonsingular, and Ackermann's formula (or the transformation method) explicitly constructs a $K$ achieving the desired placement.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation of Ackermann's Formula

We seek $K$ such that the closed-loop characteristic polynomial equals the desired one:

$$\det(sI - A + BK) = \alpha_d(s) = s^n + \alpha_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_0$$

By the **Cayley-Hamilton Theorem**, $A$ satisfies its own characteristic equation:

$$A^n + a_{n-1}A^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0 I = 0$$

The controllability matrix for a SISO system is $\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & \cdots & A^{n-1}B \end{bmatrix}$, and its inverse exists by assumption.

**Construction of the last row of $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$:**
Define $e_n^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ (a row vector of length $n$). Then:

$$e_n^T \mathcal{C}^{-1} = e_n^T \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & \cdots & A^{n-1}B \end{bmatrix}^{-1}$$

Through a sequence of linear-algebraic manipulations (multiplying by $\mathcal{C}$ from the right and identifying the result with the last row of $\mathcal{C}$'s inverse), one can show:

$$e_n^T \mathcal{C}^{-1} A^k = e_n^T \mathcal{C}^{-1} \cdot \mathcal{C} \cdot \text{(transformation)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & k\text{-th position} \end{bmatrix}$$

A more concrete approach: in controllable canonical form, $\mathcal{C} = I$, $A_c$ is the companion matrix, and $B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T$. In that case, $K_c = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha_0 - a_0 & \alpha_1 - a_1 & \cdots & \alpha_{n-1} - a_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$ places the poles directly. Transforming back gives the general formula.

**Final Statement of Ackermann's Formula:**

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_d(A)$$

where $\alpha_d(A)$ is computed as:

$$\alpha_d(A) = A^n + \alpha_{n-1}A^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_1 A + \alpha_0 I$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Illustrative — KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Given the system:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Design a state feedback gain $K$ to place the closed-loop poles at $\lambda_{1,2}^d = -1 \pm j$.

### Step 1: Verify Controllability

$$\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(\mathcal{C}) = (0)(-3) - (1)(1) = -1 \neq 0$$

So $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = 2$ — the system is controllable. ✓

### Step 2: Form the Desired Characteristic Polynomial

$$\alpha_d(s) = (s - (-1+j))(s - (-1-j)) = (s+1)^2 + 1 = s^2 + 2s + 2$$

So $\alpha_1 = 2$, $\alpha_0 = 2$.

### Step 3: Compute $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$

$$\mathcal{C}^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1}\begin{bmatrix} -3 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Compute $\alpha_d(A)$

$$\alpha_d(A) = A^2 + 2A + 2I$$

First compute $A^2$:

$$A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -3 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then:

$$2A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ -4 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 2I = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Adding them:

$$\alpha_d(A) = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -3 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ -4 & -6 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Apply Ackermann's Formula

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the first product:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 6: Verification

$$A - BK = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Characteristic polynomial: $\det(sI - (A-BK)) = s^2 + 2s + 2$ ✓ — matches the desired polynomial. The poles are $-1 \pm j$ as required.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — State Feedback Pole Placement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input r] --> SUM[+/- Summing Junction]
    SUM --> CTRL[Controller u = -Kx]
    CTRL --> PLANT[Plant G: xdot = Ax + Bu]
    PLANT --> STATE[State Vector x]
    STATE --> SENSOR[State Sensors / Observer]
    SENSOR --> FB[Feedback Path K]
    FB --> SUM
    STATE --> OUT[Output y = Cx]

    classDef ctrlClass fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef plantClass fill:#c5504b,stroke:#7a2e2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef senseClass fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef sigClass fill:#f9a825,stroke:#b06a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class CTRL,FB ctrlClass
    class PLANT,OUT plantClass
    class SENSOR senseClass
    class R,SUM,STATE sigClass
```

## 4.2 Algorithmic Flow — Pole Placement Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Given A, B, desired poles] --> C1[Compute Controllability Matrix C]
    C1 --> C2{rank C = n?}
    C2 -- No --> FAIL[STOP: System not controllable. Pole placement impossible.]
    C2 -- Yes --> S1[Compute C inverse]
    S1 --> S2[Form desired characteristic polynomial alpha_d s]
    S2 --> S3[Compute alpha_d of A = A power n + alpha terms]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Ackermann: K = e_n transpose times C inverse times alpha_d A]
    S4 --> S5[Form A minus B K]
    S5 --> S6[Verify eigenvalues of A minus B K equal desired poles]
    S6 --> S7{All match?}
    S7 -- Yes --> DONE[Design Complete: Report K]
    S7 -- No --> DEBUG[Check arithmetic and recompute]

    classDef procClass fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef decClass fill:#f9a825,stroke:#b06a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef okClass fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef errClass fill:#c5504b,stroke:#7a2e2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    class START,S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 procClass
    class C2,S7 decClass
    class DONE,OK okClass
    class FAIL,DEBUG errClass
```

## 4.3 Information-Flow Diagram — Discrete-Time (Digital) Pole Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RK[Reference r k] --> ADDSUM[Summing Node]
    ADDSUM --> ULAW[Digital Control Law u k = minus K x k]
    ULAW --> ZOH[Zero Order Hold]
    ZOH --> G[Sampled Plant: x k+1 = Phi x k + Gamma u k]
    G --> XK1[State x k+1]
    XK1 --> DELAY[Unit Delay z inverse]
    DELAY --> XK[State x k]
    XK --> FBK[Feedback K]
    XK --> ADDSUM
    XK1 --> OUT[Output y k]

    classDef digClass fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef zohClass fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#38006b,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef sigClass fill:#f9a825,stroke:#b06a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class ULAW,FBK,ADDSUM,XK,XK1 digClass
    class ZOH,G zohClass
    class RK,OUT,DELAY sigClass
```
